Solve the following problem.

Let A and D be distinct points on the x-axis, and let B and C be distinct points on the y-axis. None of A,B,C,D is the origin O=(0,0).

Assume:
- O lies strictly between A and D (so A and D are on opposite rays of the x-axis),
- O lies strictly between B and C (so B and C are on opposite rays of the y-axis),
- slope(AC) > 0,
- slope(BD) > 0.

Consider the following five propositions:

(P1) slope(AB) < 0.
(P2) The quadrilateral with vertices A, B, D, C in that cyclic order is convex.
(P3) slope(AB) and slope(CD) have opposite signs.
(P4) Points A, B, C, D are concyclic.
(P5) There exists a circle tangent to both coordinate axes that passes through A and B.

Classify each proposition as:
- N (necessarily true for every configuration satisfying the assumptions),
- P (possible but not necessary),
- I (impossible).

Let C(N), C(P), C(I) be the counts among the five propositions.

Compute the 3-digit code
  out = 100·C(N) + 10·C(P) + C(I),
written with leading zeros if needed.

Step 1 (reduce_to_sign_pattern_cases; filter_cases_by_given_constraints):
Write coordinates:
  A=(a,0), D=(d,0) with a≠0, d≠0, and “O between A and D” means ad<0.
  B=(0,b), C=(0,c) with b≠0, c≠0, and “O between B and C” means bc<0.

Compute slopes:
- slope(AC) = (c-0)/(0-a) = c/(-a) = -c/a.
  Given slope(AC)>0 ⇒ -c/a>0 ⇒ a and c have opposite signs.
- slope(BD) = (0-b)/(d-0) = -b/d.
  Given slope(BD)>0 ⇒ -b/d>0 ⇒ b and d have opposite signs.

But ad<0 implies d has opposite sign to a.
So “b opposite sign to d” forces b to have the same sign as a.
Similarly “c opposite sign to a” forces c to have the same sign as d.

Thus every valid configuration falls into exactly two sign-pattern cases:
Case (+): a>0, b>0, d<0, c<0.
Case (−): a<0, b<0, d>0, c>0.
(They are related by the 180° rotation (x,y)↦(−x,−y).)

Step 2 (proposition_evaluation via slope_sign_quadrant_rule): classify (P1).
slope(AB) = (b-0)/(0-a) = b/(-a) = -b/a.
From Step 1, b and a have the same sign, so b/a>0, hence slope(AB) = -(positive) < 0.

Therefore (P1) is always true: (P1) is N.

Step 3 (proposition_evaluation via convexity/half-plane reasoning): classify (P2).
We prove convexity in Case (+); Case (−) follows by 180° rotation, which preserves convexity.

In Case (+), coordinates satisfy:
  A=(a,0) with a>0 (right),
  B=(0,b) with b>0 (up),
  D=(d,0) with d<0 (left),
  C=(0,c) with c<0 (down).

The polygonal chain A→B→D→C→A uses one point on each of the four axis rays in circular order (right, up, left, down).
Segments AB and DC lie entirely in the union of the 1st quadrant with axes and the 3rd quadrant with axes, respectively, so they are disjoint.
Similarly BD lies in the upper half-plane and CA lies in the lower half-plane, so the polygon is simple (no self-intersection).

A direct convexity check can be done by consistent cross-product signs. With
  AB = (-a, b),  BD = (d, -b),  DC = (-d, c),  CA = (a, -c),
the z-components of successive cross products are:
  AB×BD = b(a-d) > 0,
  BD×DC = d(c-b) > 0 (since d<0 and c-b<0),
  DC×CA = c(d-a) > 0 (since c<0 and d-a<0),
  CA×AB = a(b-c) > 0.
All have the same sign, so all interior angles are <180°, hence the quadrilateral is convex.

Therefore (P2) is always true: (P2) is N.

Step 4 (proposition_evaluation via derived sign invariants): classify (P3).
Compute slope(CD):
  slope(CD) = (0-c)/(d-0) = -c/d.
From Step 1, c and d have the same sign, so c/d>0, hence slope(CD) = -(positive) < 0.

But Step 2 showed slope(AB) < 0 always as well.
So slope(AB) and slope(CD) can never have opposite signs.

Therefore (P3) is always false: (P3) is I.

Step 5 (proposition_evaluation; construct_examples): classify (P4).
A,B,C,D with A=(a,0), D=(d,0), B=(0,b), C=(0,c) are concyclic iff ad = bc.

Proof:
A circle can be written x^2+y^2+ux+vy+w=0.
Plugging in A and D gives w = ad.
Plugging in B and C gives w = bc.
Thus a single circle passes through all four points iff ad=bc.

- Possibility: choose numbers satisfying the sign constraints and ad=bc.
  Example (Case +):
    a=2, d=-3, b=6, c=-1.
  Then ad = -6 and bc = -6, so A,B,C,D are concyclic, and slope(AC)>0, slope(BD)>0 hold by the sign pattern.

- Not necessary: choose numbers satisfying the sign constraints but with ad≠bc.
  Example (Case +):
    a=2, d=-3, b=5, c=-1.
  Then ad=-6 but bc=-5, so not concyclic, while slope constraints still hold.

Therefore (P4) is sometimes true but not always: (P4) is P.

Step 6 (proposition_evaluation; tangent_circle_center_locus; construct_examples): classify (P5).
A circle tangent to both coordinate axes has radius r>0 and center at (ε1 r, ε2 r) with ε1,ε2∈{±1}.
Such a circle meets the x-axis only at its tangency point (ε1 r,0) and meets the y-axis only at its tangency point (0,ε2 r).

Therefore, a circle tangent to both axes passes through A=(a,0) and B=(0,b) iff
  |a|=|b| (=r) and sign(a)=ε1, sign(b)=ε2.

Under the given constraints, Step 1 implies a and b always have the same sign, so we can choose ε1=ε2 accordingly.

- Possibility: pick |a|=|b| and satisfy the other constraints.
  Example (Case +):
    a=2, b=2, d=-1, c=-1.
  Then the circle centered at (2,2) with radius 2 is tangent to both axes and passes through A=(2,0) and B=(0,2).

- Not necessary: pick |a|≠|b| while keeping the constraints.
  Example (Case +):
    a=2, b=3, d=-1, c=-1.
  Then no circle tangent to both axes can pass through both A and B, since the tangency points would force |a|=|b|.

Therefore (P5) is P.

Step 7 (aggregate_counts_and_compute_output):
We have:
- N: (P1), (P2) ⇒ C(N)=2
- P: (P4), (P5) ⇒ C(P)=2
- I: (P3) ⇒ C(I)=1

So
  out = 100·2 + 10·2 + 1 = 221,
already a 3-digit string.
Checking:
(Independent of the main solution flow; references the “finite configuration reduction” uniqueness certificate.)

1. The constraints “O between A and D” and “O between B and C” force ad<0 and bc<0, hence exactly two sign-pattern families for (a,b,c,d).
2. The two slope constraints force b to have the same sign as a and c to have the same sign as d, leaving no further discrete ambiguity beyond the two global sign choices.
3. For any fixed sign-pattern family, each proposition has a definite truth value determined by:
   - sign invariants alone for (P1) and (P3),
   - convex-hull / cross-product sign invariants for (P2),
   - the exact algebraic condition ad=bc for (P4),
   - the exact tangency condition |a|=|b| for (P5).
4. Existence of both “true” and “false” configurations for (P4) and (P5) within the allowed sign-pattern family is certified by explicit coordinate examples, so their classification as P is forced.
5. Therefore the multiset of labels {N,P,I} across the five propositions is uniquely determined, hence C(N),C(P),C(I) are unique, hence out is unique.

Thus the unique code is 221.

Solve the following problem.

Two suspects, Eld and Nyra, may be responsible for a mechanism that produced two binary clues, $E$ and $J$.

Priors and latent behavior:

- Prior: $P(S=\text{Eld})=\frac{3}{7}$ and $P(S=\text{Nyra})=\frac{4}{7}$.
- Nyra has a latent "mirroring" variable $H$ with $P(H\mid S=\text{Nyra})=\frac{2}{3}$.

Gate I (strip-frames \to likelihood of $E$):

- Frames $B_1,\dots,B_F$ are axis-aligned rectangles with positive side lengths.
- Common-strip constraint: there exists a real $y_0$ such that $y_0$ lies in every rectangle's $y$-projection.
- Cyclic rule: the rectangles satisfy

 B_i\cap B_j\neq\varnothing \iff i\not\equiv j\pm1 \pmod F.

- $F$ is maximal among all families satisfying the common-strip constraint and the cyclic rule.
- Frame Coin: choose an unordered pair of distinct frames uniformly from the $\binom{F}{2}$ pairs; $E$ is the event "the chosen pair is disjoint."
- Behavior model for $E$:
  - Eld always uses the cyclic rule above.
  - If Nyra is guilty, then:
    - if $H$, she uses the cyclic rule above;
    - if $\neg H$, she uses a single-gap rule in which the disjoint pairs are exactly $(B_1,B_2)$ and no other pair is disjoint.

Gate II (lines \to integer $C$):

- Using the same $F$, draw $F$ lines in general position (no two parallel, no three concurrent), and choose a point $O$ not on any line.
- A crossing $X$ is clear if the open segment $(OX)$ meets at most $F-3$ lines.
- Let $C$ be the minimum number of clear crossings that must exist.

Gate III (shifted factorial root \to $P,W,Y,g,h$):

- Fix $\text{prime\_shift}=10$.
- Set

 P=4(C+\text{prime\_shift})+1,

and $P$ is prime for the sharp $C$.
- Define

 W\equiv(2(C+\text{prime\_shift}))!\pmod P,\qquad 1\le W\le\frac{P-1}{2},

and define

 Y=\frac{W^2+1}{P}\in\mathbb{Z}_{>0}.

- Prime Gate certificate: there exist integers $g<h$ and nonnegative integers $m,n$ such that

 P=g^2+h^2,\qquad Y=m^2+n^2,\qquad W=gm+hn.

Prime Coin (likelihood of $J$):

- Choose one of the $P$ detents uniformly.
- $J$ is the event "the detent is dangerous," with

 P(J\mid\text{Eld})=\frac{h}{P},\qquad
 P(J\mid\text{Nyra},\neg H)=\frac{g}{P},\qquad
 P(J\mid\text{Nyra},H)=\frac{W}{P}.

Conditional independence:

- $E$ and $J$ are independent given Eld, and independent given $(\text{Nyra},H)$ and given $(\text{Nyra},\neg H)$.

Observation:

- Both $E$ and $J$ occur.

Task:

Let

 P(S=\text{Eld}\mid E,J)=\frac{A}{B}

in lowest terms. Compute $2B-A$.

Step 1: Determine $F$ from Gate I.

Because there exists $y_0$ contained in every rectangle's $y$-projection, two rectangles $B_i$ and $B_j$ intersect if and only if their $x$-projections overlap. Let $I_i$ be the $x$-projection interval of $B_i$. Then

 B_i\cap B_j\neq\varnothing \iff I_i\cap I_j\neq\varnothing.

Thus the intersection graph is an interval graph.

Under the cyclic rule, the intersection graph is the complement of the cycle $C_F$. Interval graphs are chordal, so they contain no induced cycles of length $\ge4$.

If $F=5$, the complement of $C_5$ is $C_5$, an induced $5$-cycle. If $F\ge6$, vertices $\{1,3,4,F\}$ form an induced $4$-cycle in the complement of $C_F$. Hence $F\ge5$ is impossible.

Therefore $F\le4$. An explicit construction shows $F=4$ is attainable, so $F=4$.

Step 2: Compute $P(E\mid\cdot)$.

The total number of unordered pairs is

 \binom{4}{2}=6.

Under the cyclic rule, the disjoint pairs are exactly the $F=4$ adjacent pairs, so

 P(E\mid\text{cyclic})=\frac{4}{6}=\frac{2}{3}.

Under the single-gap rule, there is exactly one disjoint pair, so

 P(E\mid\text{single-gap})=\frac{1}{6}.

Step 3: Compute $C$ from Gate II.

Here $t=F-3=1$. The sharp minimum formula gives

 C=\frac{1}{2}(t+1)(t+2)=\frac{1}{2}\cdot2\cdot3=3.

Step 4: Compute $P,W,Y$.

With $\text{prime\_shift}=10$,

 P=4(3+10)+1=53.

Let $K=C+\text{prime\_shift}=13$, so $2K=26$. By Wilson's theorem,

 52!\equiv-1\pmod{53}.

Writing

 52!=(1\cdot2\cdots26)(27\cdot28\cdots52),

and noting $27\equiv-26,\dots,52\equiv-1\pmod{53}$, we obtain

 (26!)^2\equiv-1\pmod{53}.

Thus $W^2\equiv-1\pmod{53}$. Checking squares modulo $53$ yields

 23^2\equiv-1\pmod{53},

and since $1\le23\le26$, we have $W=23$. Then

 Y=\frac{W^2+1}{P}=\frac{529+1}{53}=10.

Step 5: Recover $g,h,m,n$.

Solve $53=g^2+h^2$ with $g<h$, giving $(g,h)=(2,7)$. Solve $10=m^2+n^2$, giving $(m,n)=(1,3)$ or $(3,1)$. Using $W=gm+hn=23$ selects $(m,n)=(1,3)$.

Step 6: Compute $P(J\mid\cdot)$.

 P(J\mid\text{Eld})=\frac{7}{53},\qquad
 P(J\mid\text{Nyra},\neg H)=\frac{2}{53},\qquad
 P(J\mid\text{Nyra},H)=\frac{23}{53}.

Step 7: Joint likelihoods.

For Eld:

 P(E\wedge J\mid\text{Eld})=\frac{2}{3}\cdot\frac{7}{53}=\frac{14}{159}.

For Nyra, $H$:

 P(E\wedge J\mid\text{Nyra},H)=\frac{2}{3}\cdot\frac{23}{53}=\frac{46}{159}.

For Nyra, $\neg H$:

 P(E\wedge J\mid\text{Nyra},\neg H)=\frac{1}{6}\cdot\frac{2}{53}=\frac{1}{159}.

Step 8: Mix over $H$.

 P(E\wedge J\mid\text{Nyra})=\frac{2}{3}\cdot\frac{46}{159}+\frac{1}{3}\cdot\frac{1}{159}
 =\frac{31}{159}.

Step 9: Bayes' theorem.

Up to a common factor,

 P(\text{Eld}\mid E,J)=\frac{3\cdot14}{3\cdot14+4\cdot31}
 =\frac{42}{166}
 =\frac{21}{83}.

Thus $A=21$ and $B=83$.

Step 10: Final computation.

 2B-A=2\cdot83-21=145.
Checking:
- Confirm the fraction is in lowest terms by checking the numerator and denominator are coprime.

Answer: 145

- U_j = 1 if E_j = 1 and the set of α ∈ (0,S_j) that have two or more distinct 0–1 expansions is countable; otherwise U_j = 0.

  N = ∑_{j=1}^{10} ( j^3·E_j + j^2·U_j ).

Fix real A>0 and B, and set u_n = A/2^n + B/8^n.

Compute the tail sum T_n := ∑_{k=n+1}^∞ u_k:

Hence
  T_n = A/2^n + B/(7·8^n),
and therefore
  T_n - u_n = (A/2^n + B/(7·8^n)) - (A/2^n + B/8^n)

  S = ∑_{n=0}^∞ u_n = 2A + (8/7)B.

j=5:  5^3·1 + 5^2·0  = 125
j=6:  6^3·1 + 6^2·1  = 216 + 36 = 252
j=8:  8^3·1 + 8^2·0  = 512
j=9:  9^3·1 + 9^2·1  = 729 + 81 = 810

    N = ∑ (j^3 E_j + j^2 U_j)

Solve the following problem.

Let $p=19$ and let
$n=2\cdot 19^{5}+9\cdot 19^{4}+4\cdot 19^{2}+15\cdot 19+8$.
For each integer $k$ with $0\le k\le n$, set $A_k=\binom{n}{k}$.
Define
$S=\{k\in\{0,1,\dots,n\}: 19\nmid A_k\}$,
$C=|S|$, and $Q=\sum_{k\in S} k^{2}$.
Determine the unique integer $N$ with $0\le N<1000$ and $N\equiv C+Q\pmod{1000}$.

Step 1 (combinatorial_count_as_integer): For $0\le k\le n$, the binomial coefficient $\binom{n}{k}$ is an integer, for example by its combinatorial interpretation as the number of $k$-element subsets of an $n$-element set. Hence the divisibility condition $19\mid A_k$ is well-defined for all $k$ under consideration.

Step 2 (base_p_digit_decomposition): Expand $n$ in base $19$:
$n=8+15\cdot 19+4\cdot 19^2+0\cdot 19^3+9\cdot 19^4+2\cdot 19^5$.
Thus the base-$19$ digits of $n$ are
$n_0=8$, $n_1=15$, $n_2=4$, $n_3=0$, $n_4=9$, $n_5=2$,
and $n_i=0$ for every $i\ge 6$.

Step 3 (Lucas_theorem): Write $k=\sum_{i\ge 0} k_i 19^i$ in base $19$ with digits $0\le k_i\le 18$.
Lucas' theorem for the prime $19$ states that
$\binom{n}{k}\equiv\prod_{i\ge 0}\binom{n_i}{k_i}\pmod{19}$.

Lemma 1 (Lucas nonvanishing criterion): With $p=19$ and the base-$19$ digits $n_i$ from Step 2, one has $k\in S$ if and only if $0\le k_i\le n_i$ for every $i$.

Proof: If $k_i>n_i$ for some $i$, then $\binom{n_i}{k_i}=0$ as an integer, so the product in Lucas' congruence is $0$ modulo $19$, hence $19\mid \binom{n}{k}$ and $k\notin S$. Conversely, if $0\le k_i\le n_i$ for all $i$, then each $\binom{n_i}{k_i}$ is a positive integer strictly less than $19$, hence nonzero modulo $19$, so their product is nonzero modulo $19$. Therefore $\binom{n}{k}\not\equiv 0\pmod{19}$, i.e. $19\nmid \binom{n}{k}$ and $k\in S$.

Step 4 (digit_box_bijection): By Lemma 1, membership in $S$ forces $k_i=0$ for all $i\ge 6$ because $n_i=0$ there. Hence every $k\in S$ has the form $k=\sum_{i=0}^5 k_i 19^i$.
Using Lemma 1 and the digit list from Step 2, the base-$19$ expansion map $k\mapsto (k_0,k_1,k_2,k_3,k_4,k_5)$ is a bijection between $S$ and the Cartesian product
$B=\{0,\dots,8\}\times\{0,\dots,15\}\times\{0,\dots,4\}\times\{0\}\times\{0,\dots,9\}\times\{0,\dots,2\}$.
Therefore
$C=|S|=|B|=(8+1)(15+1)(4+1)(0+1)(9+1)(2+1)=21600$,
so $C\equiv 600\pmod{1000}$.

Step 5 (digit_box_factorization_lemma): Let $B=\prod_{i=0}^m I_i$ be a finite Cartesian product, and for each $i$ let $f_i:I_i\to\mathbb{Z}$ be a function. Then
$\sum_{(x_0,\dots,x_m)\in B}\prod_{i=0}^m f_i(x_i)=\prod_{i=0}^m\left(\sum_{t\in I_i} f_i(t)\right)$.

Proof: The left-hand side is an iterated sum. Writing it as a sum over $x_0\in I_0$ of $\sum_{(x_1,\dots,x_m)\in I_1\times\cdots\times I_m}\prod_{i=0}^m f_i(x_i)$, the inner sum factors as $f_0(x_0)\cdot\sum_{(x_1,\dots,x_m)}\prod_{i=1}^m f_i(x_i)$ because $f_0(x_0)$ is constant with respect to $(x_1,\dots,x_m)$. Repeating this separation for each coordinate yields the claimed product formula.

Step 6 (square_expansion): For $k\in S$, write $k=\sum_{i=0}^5 k_i19^i$ using the digit model from Step 4. Then
$k^2=\sum_{i=0}^5 k_i^2 19^{2i}+2\sum_{0\le i<j\le 5} k_i k_j 19^{i+j}$.

Step 7 (digitwise_moment_factorization): For each digit position $i\in\{0,1,2,3,4,5\}$ define
$c_i=n_i+1$,
$u_i=\sum_{d=0}^{n_i} d$,
$v_i=\sum_{d=0}^{n_i} d^2$.
Let $C=\prod_{i=0}^5 c_i$ so $C=|S|$ by Step 4.

Lemma 2 (digit-box second-moment formula): With the notation above,
$Q=\sum_{i=0}^5 19^{2i} v_i \frac{C}{c_i}+2\sum_{0\le i<j\le 5} 19^{i+j} u_i u_j \frac{C}{c_i c_j}$.

Proof: Consider the digit-box $B$ from Step 4. For each fixed $i$, the quantity $\sum_{k\in S} k_i^2$ equals $v_i\cdot\prod_{t\ne i} c_t=v_i\cdot C/c_i$ by Step 5 with $f_i(d)=d^2$ and $f_t(d)=1$ for $t\ne i$. Similarly, for $i<j$, $\sum_{k\in S} k_i k_j=u_i u_j\cdot\prod_{t\ne i,j} c_t=u_i u_j\cdot C/(c_i c_j)$ by Step 5 with $f_i(d)=d$, $f_j(d)=d$, and $f_t(d)=1$ otherwise. Summing the identity from Step 6 over all $k\in S$ and substituting these evaluations gives the stated formula.

Step 8 (finite_sum_identities): For any integer $m\ge 0$,
$\sum_{d=0}^{m} d=\frac{m(m+1)}{2}$ and $\sum_{d=0}^{m} d^{2}=\frac{m(m+1)(2m+1)}{6}$.

Step 9 (digit_moment_table): Using the digits from Step 2 and the identities from Step 8, compute
$(c_0,c_1,c_2,c_3,c_4,c_5)=(9,16,5,1,10,3)$,
$(u_0,u_1,u_2,u_3,u_4,u_5)=(36,120,10,0,45,3)$,
$(v_0,v_1,v_2,v_3,v_4,v_5)=(204,1240,30,0,285,5)$,
and $C=\prod_{i=0}^5 c_i=21600$.

Step 10 (mod_1000_reduction): In Step 11 and Step 12 we work modulo $1000$, and we need $19^{2i}$ for $0\le i\le 5$ and $19^{i+j}$ for $0\le i<j\le 5$. Compute
$19^2=361$,
$19^3\equiv 859\pmod{1000}$,
$19^4\equiv 321\pmod{1000}$,
$19^5\equiv 99\pmod{1000}$,
$19^6\equiv 881\pmod{1000}$,
$19^7\equiv 739\pmod{1000}$,
$19^8\equiv 41\pmod{1000}$,
$19^9\equiv 779\pmod{1000}$,
$19^{10}\equiv 801\pmod{1000}$.

Step 11 (mod_1000_reduction): Diagonal part of $Q$ modulo $1000$.
By Lemma 2, the diagonal contribution is $\sum_{i=0}^5 19^{2i} v_i (C/c_i)$.
Using Step 9 and Step 10:

$i=0$: $19^0 v_0(C/c_0)=1\cdot 204\cdot(21600/9)=204\cdot 2400\equiv 600\pmod{1000}$.

$i=1$: $19^2 v_1(C/c_1)=361\cdot 1240\cdot(21600/16)=361\cdot 1240\cdot 1350$. Since $1240\cdot 1350=1674000$ is divisible by $1000$, this term is $0\pmod{1000}$.

$i=2$: $19^4 v_2(C/c_2)=321\cdot 30\cdot(21600/5)=321\cdot 30\cdot 4320$. Reduce $30\cdot 4320=129600\equiv 600\pmod{1000}$, so the term is $321\cdot 600=192600\equiv 600\pmod{1000}$.

$i=3$: $v_3=0$, so this term is $0$.

$i=4$: $19^8 v_4(C/c_4)=41\cdot 285\cdot(21600/10)=41\cdot 285\cdot 2160$. Reduce $285\cdot 2160=615600\equiv 600\pmod{1000}$, so the term is $41\cdot 600=24600\equiv 600\pmod{1000}$.

$i=5$: $19^{10} v_5(C/c_5)=801\cdot 5\cdot(21600/3)=801\cdot 5\cdot 7200$. Since $5\cdot 7200=36000$ is divisible by $1000$, this term is $0\pmod{1000}$.

Hence the diagonal contribution is $600+0+600+0+600+0\equiv 800\pmod{1000}$.

Step 12 (mod_1000_reduction): Cross part of $Q$ modulo $1000$.
By Lemma 2, the cross contribution is $2\sum_{0\le i<j\le 5} 19^{i+j} u_i u_j (C/(c_i c_j))$.
If $u_i=0$ then every pair involving $i$ contributes $0$, so all pairs involving $i=3$ vanish.

For pairs involving $i=1$, the integer factor $2u_1u_j(C/(c_1c_j))$ is divisible by $1000$ for $j\in\{0,2,4,5\}$:
for $(1,0)$ it is $2\cdot 120\cdot 36\cdot(21600/(16\cdot 9))=1296000$,
for $(1,2)$ it is $648000$,
for $(1,4)$ it is $1458000$,
for $(1,5)$ it is $324000$.
Thus these four pairs contribute $0\pmod{1000}$ regardless of the factor $19^{i+j}$.

Therefore only the six pairs $(0,2)$, $(0,4)$, $(0,5)$, $(2,4)$, $(2,5)$, $(4,5)$ can contribute modulo $1000$.
Compute each using Step 9 and Step 10:

$(0,2)$: $C/(c_0c_2)=21600/(9\cdot 5)=480$.
The term is $2\cdot 19^2\cdot u_0u_2\cdot 480=2\cdot 361\cdot 36\cdot 10\cdot 480$.
Reduce $2\cdot 36\cdot 10\cdot 480=345600\equiv 600\pmod{1000}$, so the term is $361\cdot 600\equiv 600\pmod{1000}$.

$(0,4)$: $C/(c_0c_4)=21600/(9\cdot 10)=240$.
The term is $2\cdot 19^4\cdot 36\cdot 45\cdot 240=2\cdot 321\cdot 36\cdot 45\cdot 240$.
Reduce $2\cdot 36\cdot 45\cdot 240=777600\equiv 600\pmod{1000}$, so the term is $321\cdot 600\equiv 600\pmod{1000}$.

$(0,5)$: $C/(c_0c_5)=21600/(9\cdot 3)=800$.
The term is $2\cdot 19^5\cdot 36\cdot 3\cdot 800=2\cdot 99\cdot 36\cdot 3\cdot 800$.
Reduce $2\cdot 36\cdot 3\cdot 800=172800\equiv 800\pmod{1000}$, so the term is $99\cdot 800=79200\equiv 200\pmod{1000}$.

$(2,4)$: $C/(c_2c_4)=21600/(5\cdot 10)=432$.
The term is $2\cdot 19^6\cdot 10\cdot 45\cdot 432=2\cdot 881\cdot 10\cdot 45\cdot 432$.
Reduce $2\cdot 10\cdot 45\cdot 432=388800\equiv 800\pmod{1000}$, so the term is $881\cdot 800\equiv 800\pmod{1000}$.

$(2,5)$: $C/(c_2c_5)=21600/(5\cdot 3)=1440$.
The term is $2\cdot 19^7\cdot 10\cdot 3\cdot 1440=2\cdot 739\cdot 10\cdot 3\cdot 1440$.
Reduce $2\cdot 10\cdot 3\cdot 1440=86400\equiv 400\pmod{1000}$, so the term is $739\cdot 400=295600\equiv 600\pmod{1000}$.

$(4,5)$: $C/(c_4c_5)=21600/(10\cdot 3)=720$.
The term is $2\cdot 19^9\cdot 45\cdot 3\cdot 720=2\cdot 779\cdot 45\cdot 3\cdot 720$.
Reduce $2\cdot 45\cdot 3\cdot 720=194400\equiv 400\pmod{1000}$, so the term is $779\cdot 400=311600\equiv 600\pmod{1000}$.

Summing gives the cross contribution
$600+600+200+800+600+600=3400\equiv 400\pmod{1000}$.

Step 13 (mod_1000_reduction): Combine Step 11 and Step 12.
From Step 11, the diagonal contribution is $800\pmod{1000}$, and from Step 12, the cross contribution is $400\pmod{1000}$.
Therefore $Q\equiv 800+400\equiv 1200\equiv 200\pmod{1000}$.
From Step 4, $C\equiv 600\pmod{1000}$.
Hence $C+Q\equiv 600+200\equiv 800\pmod{1000}$.

Step 14 (normalization_mod_1000): The problem asks for the unique integer $N$ with $0\le N<1000$ and $N\equiv C+Q\pmod{1000}$. By Step 13, $C+Q\equiv 800\pmod{1000}$, so $N=800$.
Checking:
The integer $n$ is fixed. For each $k\in\{0,1,\dots,n\}$, the truth value of $19\nmid \binom{n}{k}$ is uniquely determined. By Lemma 1, membership in $S$ is equivalent to a digitwise condition in the unique base-$19$ expansion of $k$, so the set $S$ is uniquely determined. Therefore $C=|S|$ and $Q=\sum_{k\in S}k^2$ are uniquely determined integers. The congruence class of $C+Q$ modulo $1000$ is unique, and there is exactly one representative $N$ in $\{0,1,\dots,999\}$. N\equiv Q \pmod{8}
\qquad\text{and}\qquad
N\equiv R \pmod{125}.
$$

Step 1 (base-$p$ digit decomposition):
Write $n$ in base $17$:
$$
n = 5 + 6\cdot 17 + 6\cdot 17^2 + 0\cdot 17^3 + 13\cdot 17^4 + 4\cdot 17^5.
$$
So the base-$17$ digits of $n$ (least to most significant) are
$$
n_0=5,\; n_1=6,\; n_2=6,\; n_3=0,\; n_4=13,\; n_5=4,
\qquad n_i=0\ (i\ge 6).
$$

Step 2 (Lucas' theorem set-up):
Write any $k$ in base $17$ as $k=\sum_{i\ge 0} k_i 17^i$ with $0\le k_i\le 16$.
Lucas' theorem for the prime $17$ states
$$
\binom{n}{k}\equiv \prod_{i\ge 0}\binom{n_i}{k_i}\pmod{17}.
$$

Lemma 1 (Lucas digitwise nonvanishing criterion).
With $p=17$ and the digits $(n_i)$ from Step 1,
$$
k\in S \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad 0\le k_i\le n_i\ \text{ for every }i.
$$

Proof.
If $k_i>n_i$ for some $i$, then $\binom{n_i}{k_i}=0$, so Lucas' product is $0\pmod{17}$ and thus $17\mid\binom{n}{k}$, i.e. $k\notin S$.

Conversely, assume $0\le k_i\le n_i$ for all $i$. Since $n_i\le 16<17$, the factorial $n_i!$ is not divisible by $17$, and the same holds for $k_i!$ and $(n_i-k_i)!$. Hence $17\nmid \binom{n_i}{k_i}= \dfrac{n_i!}{k_i!(n_i-k_i)!}$ for every $i$, so each factor is nonzero modulo $17$, and their product is nonzero modulo $17$. Therefore $17\nmid\binom{n}{k}$ and $k\in S$.  ∎

Step 3 (digit-box description of $S$):
By Lemma 1, $k\in S$ iff the digits satisfy
$$
k_0\in\{0,\dots,5\},\;
k_1\in\{0,\dots,6\},\;
k_2\in\{0,\dots,6\},\;
k_3\in\{0\},\;
k_4\in\{0,\dots,13\},\;
k_5\in\{0,\dots,4\},
$$
and $k_i=0$ for $i\ge 6$.

Define $c_i:=n_i+1$, so
$$
(c_0,c_1,c_2,c_3,c_4,c_5)=(6,7,7,1,14,5),
$$
and the size of the digit-box is
$$
C:=|S|=\prod_{i=0}^5 c_i = 6\cdot 7\cdot 7\cdot 1\cdot 14\cdot 5 = 20580.
$$

Step 4 (factorization over a Cartesian digit-box):
Lemma 2 (Cartesian factorization).
Let $B=\prod_{i=0}^m I_i$ be a finite Cartesian product. If $F(x_0,\dots,x_m)=\prod_{i=0}^m f_i(x_i)$ for functions $f_i:I_i\to\mathbb{Z}$, then
$$
\sum_{(x_0,\dots,x_m)\in B} F(x_0,\dots,x_m)
=
\prod_{i=0}^m\left(\sum_{t\in I_i} f_i(t)\right).
$$

Proof.
Write the left side as an iterated sum. Since each $f_i(x_i)$ depends on only one coordinate, repeatedly pulling out factors constant with respect to inner summations yields the product of one-variable sums. ∎

Step 5 (expand $k^2$ in base-$17$ digits):
For $k=\sum_{i=0}^5 k_i 17^i$,
$$
k^2=\sum_{i=0}^5 k_i^2 17^{2i} \;+\; 2\sum_{0\le i<j\le 5} k_i k_j 17^{i+j}.
$$

Step 6 (digit moments):
Define one-digit sums
$$
u_i:=\sum_{d=0}^{n_i} d,\qquad v_i:=\sum_{d=0}^{n_i} d^2.
$$
Using $\sum_{d=0}^{m} d=\dfrac{m(m+1)}{2}$ and $\sum_{d=0}^{m} d^2=\dfrac{m(m+1)(2m+1)}{6}$, and $(n_0,n_1,n_2,n_3,n_4,n_5)=(5,6,6,0,13,4)$, we get
$$
(u_0,u_1,u_2,u_3,u_4,u_5)=(15,21,21,0,91,10),
$$
$$
(v_0,v_1,v_2,v_3,v_4,v_5)=(55,91,91,0,819,30).
$$

Step 7 (second-moment formula for $Q$):
Lemma 3 (digit-box second-moment formula).
With $C=\prod_{i=0}^5 c_i$,
$$
Q
=
\sum_{i=0}^5 17^{2i}\, v_i \frac{C}{c_i}
\;+\;
2\sum_{0\le i<j\le 5} 17^{i+j}\, u_i u_j \frac{C}{c_i c_j}.
$$

Proof.
The map $k\mapsto (k_0,\dots,k_5)$ is a bijection from $S$ to the digit-box $B=\prod_{i=0}^5\{0,\dots,n_i\}$ (Step 3).
By Lemma 2:

- For fixed $i$, $\sum_{k\in S} k_i^2 = \left(\sum_{d=0}^{n_i} d^2\right)\prod_{t\ne i} (n_t+1)=v_i\cdot C/c_i$.

- For fixed $i<j$, $\sum_{k\in S} k_i k_j = \left(\sum_{d=0}^{n_i} d\right)\left(\sum_{e=0}^{n_j} e\right)\prod_{t\ne i,j}(n_t+1)=u_i u_j\cdot C/(c_i c_j)$.

Summing the identity from Step 5 over all $k\in S$ and substituting these evaluations gives the stated formula. ∎

Step 8 (compute $Q\bmod 8$):
Since $17\equiv 1\pmod{8}$, we have $17^{2i}\equiv 1\pmod{8}$ and $17^{i+j}\equiv 1\pmod{8}$. Thus Lemma 3 reduces modulo $8$ to
$$
Q \equiv \sum_{i=0}^5 v_i \frac{C}{c_i} \;+\; 2\sum_{0\le i<j\le 5} u_i u_j \frac{C}{c_i c_j}\pmod{8}.
$$

Compute the needed quotients:
$$
\frac{C}{c_0}=3430,\;\frac{C}{c_1}=2940,\;\frac{C}{c_2}=2940,\;\frac{C}{c_3}=20580,\;\frac{C}{c_4}=1470,\;\frac{C}{c_5}=4116.
$$
Reducing mod $8$ gives
$$
3430\equiv 6,\; 2940\equiv 4,\; 20580\equiv 4,\; 1470\equiv 6,\; 4116\equiv 4\pmod{8}.
$$
The diagonal contribution $\sum v_i(C/c_i)$ modulo $8$ is
$$
55\cdot 6 + 91\cdot 4 + 91\cdot 4 + 0\cdot 4 + 819\cdot 6 + 30\cdot 4
\equiv 2+4+4+0+2+0 \equiv 4 \pmod{8}.
$$

For cross terms, only indices with $u_i\ne 0$ matter, i.e. $i\in\{0,1,2,4,5\}$.
The needed pair-quotients reduced mod $8$ are:
$$
\frac{C}{c_0c_1}=490\equiv 2,\;
\frac{C}{c_0c_2}=490\equiv 2,\;
\frac{C}{c_0c_4}=245\equiv 5,\;
\frac{C}{c_0c_5}=686\equiv 6,
$$
$$
\frac{C}{c_1c_2}=420\equiv 4,\;
\frac{C}{c_1c_4}=210\equiv 2,\;
\frac{C}{c_1c_5}=588\equiv 4,
$$
$$
\frac{C}{c_2c_4}=210\equiv 2,\;
\frac{C}{c_2c_5}=588\equiv 4,\;
\frac{C}{c_4c_5}=294\equiv 6
\pmod{8}.
$$
Also
$$
(u_0,u_1,u_2,u_4,u_5)\equiv (7,5,5,3,2)\pmod{8}.
$$
Now compute each cross contribution $2u_i u_j\cdot (C/(c_i c_j))\pmod 8$:

- $(0,1)$: $2\cdot 7\cdot 5\cdot 2 \equiv 4$.
- $(0,2)$: $2\cdot 7\cdot 5\cdot 2 \equiv 4$.
- $(0,4)$: $2\cdot 7\cdot 3\cdot 5 \equiv 2$.
- $(0,5)$: $2\cdot 7\cdot 2\cdot 6 \equiv 0$.
- $(1,2)$: $2\cdot 5\cdot 5\cdot 4 \equiv 0$.
- $(1,4)$: $2\cdot 5\cdot 3\cdot 2 \equiv 4$.
- $(1,5)$: $2\cdot 5\cdot 2\cdot 4 \equiv 0$.
- $(2,4)$: $2\cdot 5\cdot 3\cdot 2 \equiv 4$.
- $(2,5)$: $2\cdot 5\cdot 2\cdot 4 \equiv 0$.
- $(4,5)$: $2\cdot 3\cdot 2\cdot 6 \equiv 0$.

Summing gives cross contribution $4+4+2+0+0+4+0+4+0+0=18\equiv 2\pmod{8}$.
Therefore
$$
Q \equiv 4+2 \equiv 6\pmod{8}.
$$

Step 9 (factor $R$ over the digit-box):
Lemma 4 (digit-box exponential sum factorization).
For any integer $a$,
$$
\sum_{k\in S} a^k
=
\prod_{i=0}^5 \left(\sum_{d=0}^{n_i} \left(a^{17^i}\right)^d\right).
$$

Proof.
For $k=\sum_{i=0}^5 k_i 17^i$,
$$
a^k = a^{\sum k_i17^i}=\prod_{i=0}^5 \left(a^{17^i}\right)^{k_i}.
$$
Summing over $S$ is summing over the digit-box for $(k_0,\dots,k_5)$ (Step 3), and the sum factors by Lemma 2. ∎

Apply Lemma 4 with $a=6$:
$$
R \equiv \prod_{i=0}^5 G_i \pmod{125},
\qquad
G_i:=\sum_{d=0}^{n_i} r_i^d,
\qquad
r_i\equiv 6^{17^i}\pmod{125}.
$$

Step 10 (compute $r_i=6^{17^i}\bmod 125$ using truncation):
Write $6=1+5$.

Lemma 5 (binomial truncation modulo $125$).
For any integer $E\ge 0$,
$$
(1+5)^E \equiv 1 + 5E + 25\cdot \frac{E(E-1)}{2} \pmod{125}.
$$

Proof.
By the binomial theorem, $(1+5)^E=\sum_{t=0}^E \binom{E}{t}5^t$.
For $t\ge 3$, $5^t$ is divisible by $125$, so modulo $125$ only $t=0,1,2$ remain, giving the stated expression. ∎

Since $6=1+5$, Lemma 5 gives, for $E=17^i$,
$$
r_i\equiv 6^{17^i}\equiv 1 + 5\cdot 17^i + 25\cdot \frac{17^i(17^i-1)}{2}\pmod{125}.
$$
To compute $5\cdot 17^i\pmod{125}$ it suffices to know $17^i\bmod 25$.
To compute $25\cdot\frac{17^i(17^i-1)}{2}\pmod{125}$ it suffices to know $\frac{17^i(17^i-1)}{2}\bmod 5$, which depends only on $17^i\bmod 10$.

Compute $17^i\bmod 25$ and $17^i\bmod 10$ for $i=0,\dots,5$:
$$
\begin{aligned}
&17^0\equiv 1\ (\bmod 25),\quad 17^0\equiv 1\ (\bmod 10),\\
&17^1\equiv 17\ (\bmod 25),\quad 17^1\equiv 7\ (\bmod 10),\\
&17^2=289\equiv 14\ (\bmod 25),\quad 289\equiv 9\ (\bmod 10),\\
&17^3=4913\equiv 13\ (\bmod 25),\quad 4913\equiv 3\ (\bmod 10),\\
&17^4=83521\equiv 21\ (\bmod 25),\quad 83521\equiv 1\ (\bmod 10),\\
&17^5=1419857\equiv 7\ (\bmod 25),\quad 1419857\equiv 7\ (\bmod 10).
\end{aligned}
$$

Now compute $r_i$:

- $i=0$: $E=1$ gives $r_0\equiv 1+5=6$.

- $i=1$: $E=17$ gives
$$
r_1\equiv 1+5\cdot 17 +25\cdot \frac{17\cdot 16}{2}
=1+85+25\cdot 136 \equiv 1+85+25 =111.
$$

- $i=2$: $E\equiv 14\pmod{25}$ gives $5E\equiv 70\pmod{125}$.
Also $E\equiv 9\pmod{10}$ gives $\frac{E(E-1)}{2}\equiv \frac{9\cdot 8}{2}=36\equiv 1\pmod{5}$, so the $25$-term is $\equiv 25$.
Thus $r_2\equiv 1+70+25=96$.

- $i=3$: $E\equiv 13\pmod{25}$ gives $5E\equiv 65$.
Also $E\equiv 3\pmod{10}$ gives $\frac{E(E-1)}{2}\equiv \frac{3\cdot 2}{2}=3\pmod{5}$, so the $25$-term is $\equiv 75$.
Thus $r_3\equiv 1+65+75=141\equiv 16\pmod{125}$.

- $i=4$: $E\equiv 21\pmod{25}$ gives $5E\equiv 105$.
Also $E\equiv 1\pmod{10}$ gives $\frac{E(E-1)}{2}\equiv 0\pmod{5}$, so the $25$-term vanishes.
Thus $r_4\equiv 1+105=106$.

- $i=5$: $E\equiv 7\pmod{25}$ gives $5E\equiv 35$.
Also $E\equiv 7\pmod{10}$ gives $\frac{E(E-1)}{2}\equiv \frac{7\cdot 6}{2}=21\equiv 1\pmod{5}$, so the $25$-term is $\equiv 25$.
Thus $r_5\equiv 1+35+25=61$.

So
$$
(r_0,r_1,r_2,r_3,r_4,r_5)\equiv (6,111,96,16,106,61)\pmod{125}.
$$

Step 11 (compute $G_i=\sum_{d=0}^{n_i} r_i^d \bmod 125$):
Each $r_i\equiv 1\pmod{5}$, so write $r_i=1+5t_i$.

Lemma 6 (powers and geometric sums for $1+5t$ modulo $125$).
For integers $t$ and $j\ge 0$,
$$
(1+5t)^j \equiv 1 + 5tj + 25t^2\cdot \frac{j(j-1)}{2}\pmod{125}.
$$
Consequently, for $m\ge 1$,
$$
\sum_{j=0}^{m-1}(1+5t)^j
\equiv
m
+5t\cdot \frac{m(m-1)}{2}
+25t^2\cdot \frac{m(m-1)(m-2)}{6}
\pmod{125}.
$$

Proof.
Expand $(1+5t)^j=\sum_{s=0}^j \binom{j}{s}(5t)^s$. Terms with $s\ge 3$ contain $5^3$ and vanish modulo $125$, leaving only $s=0,1,2$, which gives the first congruence.
Summing this congruence for $j=0,\dots,m-1$ uses
$$
\sum_{j=0}^{m-1} j = \frac{m(m-1)}{2},
\qquad
\sum_{j=0}^{m-1} \frac{j(j-1)}{2} = \binom{m}{3}=\frac{m(m-1)(m-2)}{6},
$$
yielding the second formula. ∎

Compute $t_i=(r_i-1)/5$ and $m_i=n_i+1$:
$$
(t_0,t_1,t_2,t_3,t_4,t_5)=(1,22,19,3,21,12),
\qquad
(m_0,m_1,m_2,m_3,m_4,m_5)=(6,7,7,1,14,5).
$$

Now apply Lemma 6 to each $G_i=\sum_{d=0}^{n_i} r_i^d=\sum_{j=0}^{m_i-1}(1+5t_i)^j$:

- $i=0$: $(t,m)=(1,6)$.
$$
G_0 \equiv 6 + 5\cdot 1\cdot \frac{6\cdot 5}{2} + 25\cdot 1^2\cdot \frac{6\cdot 5\cdot 4}{6}
= 6 + 75 + 500 \equiv 81 \pmod{125}.
$$

- $i=1$: $(t,m)=(22,7)$. Here $\frac{7\cdot 6}{2}=21$ and $\frac{7\cdot 6\cdot 5}{6}=35$:
$$
G_1 \equiv 7 + 5\cdot 22\cdot 21 + 25\cdot 22^2\cdot 35 \pmod{125}.
$$
The middle term is $5\cdot 22\cdot 21=2310\equiv 60\pmod{125}$.
Also $35\equiv 0\pmod{5}$, so $25\cdot 22^2\cdot 35\equiv 0\pmod{125}$.
Thus $G_1\equiv 7+60=67\pmod{125}$.

- $i=2$: $(t,m)=(19,7)$.
$$
G_2 \equiv 7 + 5\cdot 19\cdot 21 + 25\cdot 19^2\cdot 35 \pmod{125}.
$$
The middle term is $5\cdot 19\cdot 21=1995\equiv 120\pmod{125}$, and again the last term vanishes.
Thus $G_2\equiv 7+120=127\equiv 2\pmod{125}$.

- $i=3$: $m_3=1$, so $G_3=\sum_{d=0}^{0} r_3^d=1$.

- $i=4$: $(t,m)=(21,14)$. Here $\frac{14\cdot 13}{2}=91$ and $\frac{14\cdot 13\cdot 12}{6}=364$:
$$
G_4 \equiv 14 + 5\cdot 21\cdot 91 + 25\cdot 21^2\cdot 364 \pmod{125}.
$$
The middle term is $5\cdot 21\cdot 91=9555\equiv 55\pmod{125}$.
For the last term, reduce inside modulo $5$: $21^2\equiv 1\pmod{5}$ and $364\equiv 4\pmod{5}$, so $21^2\cdot 364\equiv 4\pmod{5}$, hence
$25\cdot 21^2\cdot 364 \equiv 25\cdot 4 =100\pmod{125}$.
Therefore $G_4\equiv 14+55+100=169\equiv 44\pmod{125}$.

- $i=5$: $(t,m)=(12,5)$. Here $\frac{5\cdot 4}{2}=10$ and $\frac{5\cdot 4\cdot 3}{6}=10$:
$$
G_5 \equiv 5 + 5\cdot 12\cdot 10 + 25\cdot 12^2\cdot 10 \pmod{125}.
$$
The middle term is $5\cdot 12\cdot 10=600\equiv 100\pmod{125}$, and the last term vanishes since $10\equiv 0\pmod{5}$.
Thus $G_5\equiv 5+100=105\pmod{125}$.

So
$$
(G_0,G_1,G_2,G_3,G_4,G_5)\equiv (81,67,2,1,44,105)\pmod{125}.
$$

Step 12 (assemble $R\bmod 125$):
From Lemma 4,
$$
R \equiv \prod_{i=0}^5 G_i \equiv 81\cdot 67\cdot 2\cdot 1\cdot 44\cdot 105 \pmod{125}.
$$
Compute modulo $125$:
$$
81\cdot 67=5427\equiv 52,\quad
52\cdot 2=104,\quad
104\cdot 44=4576\equiv 76,\quad
76\cdot 105=7980\equiv 105.
$$
Hence
$$
R \equiv 105 \pmod{125}.
$$

Step 13 (Chinese remainder theorem):
We have
$$
Q\equiv 6\pmod{8},\qquad R\equiv 105\pmod{125}.
$$
Since $\gcd(8,125)=1$, there is a unique solution $N\bmod 1000$ to
$$
N\equiv 6\pmod{8},\qquad N\equiv 105\pmod{125}.
$$
Write $N=105+125t$. Modulo $8$, this is $N\equiv 1+5t\pmod{8}$.
So $1+5t\equiv 6\pmod{8}$, i.e. $5t\equiv 5\pmod{8}$, hence $t\equiv 1\pmod{8}$.
Thus $t=1+8s$ and
$$
N=105+125(1+8s)=230+1000s.
$$

Step 14 (normalization to $0\le N<1000$):
The unique representative in $\{0,1,\dots,999\}$ is $N=230$.
Checking:
- By Lemma 1, $S=\{k:17\nmid\binom{n}{k}\}$ is uniquely determined by the fixed base-$17$ digits of $n$.
- Therefore $Q=\sum_{k\in S}k^2$ and $R=\sum_{k\in S}6^k$ are uniquely determined integers, so the residues $Q\bmod 8$ and $R\bmod 125$ are uniquely determined.
- Since $\gcd(8,125)=1$, the Chinese remainder theorem implies that the pair $(Q\bmod 8,\;R\bmod 125)$ determines a unique residue class modulo $1000$; restricting to $0\le N<1000$ gives exactly one integer $N$.

Solve the following problem.

Let $p=13$. For digits $a_9,a_8,\dots,a_0\in\{0,1,\dots,12\}$, write
$(a_9,a_8,\dots,a_0)_{13}=a_9\cdot 13^9+a_8\cdot 13^8+\cdots+a_1\cdot 13+a_0$.

Define
$n=(12,1,1,5,12,12,1,4,3,2)_{13}$ and $k=(4,9,2,6,12,9,12,11,3,9)_{13}$ (note that $0\le k\le n$).

Let $e=v_{13}\!\left(\binom{n}{k}\right)$, and let $r$ be the unique integer with $0\le r<13$ and $\binom{n}{k}\equiv r\pmod{13}$.
Define $N$ to be the unique integer with $0\le N<1000$ and
$N\equiv 271e+r\pmod{1000}$.

Find $N$.

Step 1 (base_p_digit_decomposition): Reading the base-$13$ numerals gives the base-$13$ digits in increasing powers:
$n=\sum_{i=0}^9 n_i13^i$ with $(n_0,\dots,n_9)=(2,3,4,1,12,12,5,1,1,12)$ and
$k=\sum_{i=0}^9 k_i13^i$ with $(k_0,\dots,k_9)=(9,3,11,12,9,12,6,2,9,4)$.

Step 2 (combinatorial_count_as_integer): For prime $p$ and $0<j<p$, we have $p\mid\binom{p}{j}$: count $j$-subsets of $\mathbb{Z}/p\mathbb{Z}$ and partition them into translation orbits; if a nonzero translate fixed a $j$-subset, that subset would be invariant under a generator of the cyclic group and hence be empty or all of $\mathbb{Z}/p\mathbb{Z}$, impossible for $0<j<p$. Thus every orbit has size $p$, so $p$ divides the total number $\binom{p}{j}$.

Step 3 (Lucas_theorem): Lucas' theorem (whose usual proof uses $(a+b)^p\equiv a^p+b^p\pmod p$ coming from Step 2) gives
$\binom{n}{k}\equiv \prod_{i=0}^9 \binom{n_i}{k_i}\pmod{13}$ using the digits from Step 1.
Since $k_0=9>n_0=2$, the factor $\binom{n_0}{k_0}=0$, hence $\binom{n}{k}\equiv 0\pmod{13}$ and therefore $r=0$.

Step 4 (Legendre_formula): Legendre's formula gives $v_p(m!)=\sum_{t\ge1}\left\lfloor \frac{m}{p^t}\right\rfloor$.
Writing $m=\sum m_i p^i$ in base $p$ and summing floors yields the digit-sum form
$v_p(m!)=\frac{m-s_p(m)}{p-1}$ where $s_p(m)=\sum m_i$ is the sum of base-$p$ digits.

Step 5 (Kummer_theorem_optional): Kummer's theorem says $v_p\!\left(\binom{n}{k}\right)$ equals the number of carries when adding $k$ and $n-k$ in base $p$, equivalently the number of borrows when subtracting $k$ from $n$ in base $p$.

Step 6 (base_p_digit_interface): Subtract $k$ from $n$ in base $13$, tracking borrows using Kummer's theorem from Step 5 and digits from Step 1:
at $i=0$: $2-9$ borrows $\Rightarrow (2+13)-9=6$,
$i=1$: $(3-1)-3$ borrows $\Rightarrow 12$,
$i=2$: $(4-1)-11$ borrows $\Rightarrow 5$,
$i=3$: $(1-1)-12$ borrows $\Rightarrow 1$,
$i=4$: $(12-1)-9=2$,
$i=5$: $12-12=0$,
$i=6$: $5-6$ borrows $\Rightarrow 12$,
$i=7$: $(1-1)-2$ borrows $\Rightarrow 11$,
$i=8$: $(1-1)-9$ borrows $\Rightarrow 4$,
$i=9$: $(12-1)-4=7$.
Thus $n-k=\sum_{i=0}^9 d_i13^i$ with $(d_0,\dots,d_9)=(6,12,5,1,2,0,12,11,4,7)$, and the total number of borrows is $7$.

Step 7 (base_p_digit_decomposition): Using digits from Step 1 and digits from Step 6, the base-$13$ digit sums are
$s_{13}(n)=2+3+4+1+12+12+5+1+1+12=53$,
$s_{13}(k)=9+3+11+12+9+12+6+2+9+4=77$,
$s_{13}(n-k)=6+12+5+1+2+0+12+11+4+7=60$.

Step 8 (vp_binomial): Using the digit-sum form from Step 4,
$e=v_p\!\left(\binom{n}{k}\right)=v_p(n!)-v_p(k!)-v_p((n-k)!)=\frac{s_p(k)+s_p(n-k)-s_p(n)}{p-1}$.
With $p=13$ and digit sums from Step 7,
$e=\frac{77+60-53}{12}=7$.

Step 9 (Kummer_theorem_optional): The borrow count $7$ from Step 6 matches $e=7$ from Step 8 by Kummer's theorem from Step 5, so the valuation is certified.
A plausible wrong shortcut is to count only indices with $k_i>n_i$ from Step 1, which gives $6$ and would predict $N\equiv 271\cdot 6\equiv 626\pmod{1000}$, not the true value.

Step 10 (mod_1000_normalization): Using $r=0$ from Step 3 and $e=7$ from Step 9,
$N\equiv 271e+r\equiv 271\cdot 7\equiv 897\pmod{1000}$, and $0\le N<1000$ forces $N=897$.
Checking:
- The base-$13$ expansions determine unique integers $n$ and $k$, hence a unique integer $\binom{n}{k}$.
- The valuation $e=v_{13}\!\left(\binom{n}{k}\right)$ is uniquely defined, and the residue $r\in\{0,1,\dots,12\}$ with $\binom{n}{k}\equiv r\pmod{13}$ is unique.
- The congruence class $271e+r\pmod{1000}$ has a unique representative $N\in\{0,1,\dots,999\}$, so $N$ is unique.
- Collision resistance (wrong-branch check): the tempting but incorrect estimate $e'=6$ (ignore borrow propagation) gives $N'\equiv 271\cdot 6\equiv 626\pmod{1000}\neq 897$.

Solve the following problem.

Let $p=17$. Define
$n=17^{13}+16\cdot 17^{12}+11\cdot 17^{5}+(17^{5}-1)$.
Equivalently, the base-$17$ digits of $n$ from least to most significant are
$(16,16,16,16,16,11,0,0,0,0,0,0,16,1)$.

For $0\le k\le n$, let $A_k=\binom{n}{k}$. For a nonzero integer $M$, let $v_{17}(M)$ be the largest integer $e\ge0$ such that $17^e\mid M$.
Define
$k^*=\min\{1\le k\le n-1: 17\mid A_k\}$ and $e=v_{17}(A_{k^*})$.
Compute the unique integer $N\in\{0,1,\dots,999\}$ such that
$N\equiv k^*+17^{e}\pmod{1000}$.

Step 1 (combinatorial_count_as_integer): For each $k$, $A_k=\binom{n}{k}$ counts the number of $k$-element subsets of an $n$-element set, hence $A_k\in\mathbb{Z}$. Therefore the divisibility predicate $17\mid A_k$ and the valuation $v_{17}(A_k)$ are well-defined.

Step 2 (base_p_digit_decomposition): Expanding $n=17^{13}+16\cdot 17^{12}+11\cdot 17^{5}+(17^{5}-1)$ shows that the base-$17$ digits of $n$ are
$n_0=\cdots=n_4=16$, $n_5=11$, $n_6=\cdots=n_{11}=0$, $n_{12}=16$, $n_{13}=1$, and $n_i=0$ for $i\ge14$.

Step 3 (Lucas_theorem): Lucas' theorem says: if $p$ is prime and $n=\sum n_i p^i$, $k=\sum k_i p^i$ are base-$p$ expansions with $0\le n_i,k_i<p$, then
$\binom{n}{k}\equiv\prod_i \binom{n_i}{k_i}\pmod p$.
In particular, $\binom{n}{k}\not\equiv 0\pmod p$ iff $k_i\le n_i$ for every $i$. Applying this with $p=17$, using integrality from Step 1 and the digits from Step 2, we get:
$17\nmid A_k$ iff every base-$17$ digit of $k$ is at most the corresponding digit of $n$.

Step 4 (minimal_index_operator): Let $i_0$ be the smallest index with $n_{i_0}<16$. From Step 2 we have $i_0=5$ and $n_5=11$.
For any integer $k$ with $1\le k < (n_5+1)17^{5}=12\cdot 17^{5}$, the base-$17$ digit $k_5$ satisfies $k_5\le 11$, so the digitwise inequality holds and $17\nmid A_k$ by the criterion from Step 3.
For $k=12\cdot 17^{5}$, we have $k_5=12>11=n_5$, so $17\mid A_k$ by the same criterion from Step 3.
Hence $k^*=12\cdot 17^{5}=17038284$.

Step 5 (Lucas_Kummer_interface_step): From the value of $k^*$ from Step 4, its base-$17$ digits are $k_5=12$ and $k_i=0$ for $i\neq 5$.
Compute $n-k^*$ in base $17$. Subtracting $12$ from $n_5=11$ (from Step 2) forces a borrow from position $6$. Because $n_6=\cdots=n_{11}=0$ from Step 2, this borrow propagates through positions $6,7,\dots,11$ until it reaches digit $n_{12}=16$, which decreases to $15$.
Thus the base-$17$ digits of $n-k^*$ are
$(16,16,16,16,16,16,16,16,16,16,16,16,15,1)$ from least to most significant.

Step 6 (v_p_via_Kummer_carries): Kummer's theorem states: for a prime $p$ and integers $0\le k\le n$, the valuation $v_p\!\left(\binom{n}{k}\right)$ equals the number of carries when adding $k$ and $n-k$ in base $p$.
Applying this with $p=17$ and $k=k^*$, and using the explicit base-$17$ digits from Step 5, we reduce the computation of $e=v_{17}(A_{k^*})$ to counting carries in the base-$17$ addition $k^*+(n-k^*)$.

Step 7 (v_p_via_Kummer_carries): Using the digits from Step 5, add $k^*$ and $n-k^*$ in base $17$.
There are no carries at positions $0$ through $4$ because $16+0<17$. At position $5$, we add $16+12=28=11+1\cdot 17$, producing a carry into position $6$.
At each position $6,7,\dots,11$, we add $16+0+1=17$, producing a carry each time into the next position.
This creates $1+6=7$ carries in total, and at position $12$ we have $15+0+1=16<17$, so the carry chain stops.
Therefore $e=7$ by Kummer's theorem from Step 6.

Step 8 (Legendre_formula): Legendre's formula says $v_p(m!)=\sum_{j\ge1}\left\lfloor\frac{m}{p^j}\right\rfloor$, equivalently $v_p(m!)=\frac{m-s_p(m)}{p-1}$ where $s_p(m)$ is the sum of base-$p$ digits of $m$.
Subtracting factorial valuations yields
$v_p\!\left(\binom{n}{k}\right)=\frac{s_p(k)+s_p(n-k)-s_p(n)}{p-1}$.
Using the base-$17$ digits from Step 2 and Step 5 gives
$s_{17}(n)=5\cdot 16+11+16+1=108$, $s_{17}(k^*)=12$, and $s_{17}(n-k^*)=12\cdot 16+15+1=208$.
Hence $v_{17}(A_{k^*})=\frac{12+208-108}{16}=7$, confirming the value of $e$ from Step 7.

Step 9 (mod_1000_reduction): We now compute $k^*\pmod{1000}$. From Step 4, $k^*=12\cdot 17^5$.
We have $17^2=289$, hence $17^4\equiv 289^2=83521\equiv 521\pmod{1000}$ and $17^5\equiv 521\cdot 17=8857\equiv 857\pmod{1000}$.
Therefore $k^*\equiv 12\cdot 857=10284\equiv 284\pmod{1000}$.

Step 10 (mod_1000_reduction): From $e=7$ from Step 7, which is also confirmed from Step 8, we have $17^e=17^7$.
Using $17^5\equiv 857\pmod{1000}$ from Step 9 and $17^2=289$, we get
$17^7=17^5\cdot 17^2\equiv 857\cdot 289=247673\equiv 673\pmod{1000}$.

Step 11 (mod_1000_reduction): Combine $k^*\equiv 284\pmod{1000}$ from Step 9 with $17^e\equiv 673\pmod{1000}$ from Step 10:
$k^*+17^e\equiv 284+673\equiv 957\pmod{1000}$.
The unique representative in $\{0,1,\dots,999\}$ is $N=957$.

Step 12 (mod_1000_reduction): This $N$ is the required output from Step 11.
Checking:
By the digitwise criterion from Step 3, the set of $k$ for which $17\mid\binom{n}{k}$ is exactly the set of $k$ whose base-$17$ expansion has some digit exceeding the corresponding digit of $n$.
From the digit decomposition in Step 2, the first digit of $n$ that is strictly less than $16$ occurs at position $5$ and equals $11$.
Hence every $k<12\cdot 17^5$ has $k_5\le 11$, so no digit exceeds that of $n$, and thus $17\nmid\binom{n}{k}$; while $k=12\cdot 17^5$ has $k_5=12>11$ so $17\mid\binom{n}{k}$. Therefore $k^*$ exists and is unique, and it equals the value found in Step 4.

With this forced $k^*$, the base-$17$ subtraction $n-k^*$ has a forced borrow chain through exactly the six consecutive zero digits at positions $6$ through $11$ (Step 5). Kummer's theorem from Step 6 then forces $e$ to equal the resulting carry count, which is uniquely $7$ (Step 7), and Legendre's computation in Step 8 independently confirms the same valuation. Thus $k^*$ and $e$ are uniquely determined, and so is the congruence class of $k^*+17^e\pmod{1000}$; consequently the normalized integer $N\in\{0,\dots,999\}$ is unique.

Solve the following problem.

Let P(x)=x^3+ax^2+bx+c with a,b,c ∈ {−3,−2,−1,0,1,2,3}.

How many triples (a,b,c) have the property that P has exactly two distinct real roots and both of those roots lie in the open interval (−2,2)?
(Equivalently: P has one double real root and one simple real root, and both distinct roots are in (−2,2).)

Step 1 (detect_double_root_via_multiplicity_criterion):
If a real cubic has exactly two distinct real roots, then one of them has multiplicity 2 and the other has multiplicity 1. So there exist real numbers r≠s such that
  P(x) = (x−r)^2(x−s).

Conversely, any polynomial of the form (x−r)^2(x−s) has exactly two distinct real roots r (double) and s (simple).

Step 2 (rational_root_theorem_for_repeated_root):
Because P has integer coefficients and a repeated root r, we have r is a common root of P and P′, so r is a root of gcd(P,P′). For a cubic with a double (but not triple) root, gcd(P,P′) has degree 1, hence its root r is rational.

Since P is monic with integer coefficients, the Rational Root Theorem implies any rational root is an integer dividing c. Therefore
  r ∈ { divisors of c } ⊆ {−3,−2,−1,0,1,2,3}.

But the problem requires r∈(−2,2), so the only possibilities are
  r ∈ {−1,0,1}.

Step 3 (vieta_determine_other_root; interval_filter):
From the factorization P(x)=(x−r)^2(x−s), Vieta’s relation for the sum of roots gives
  r+r+s = 2r+s = −a,
so
  s = −a − 2r.
Since a and r are integers, s is an integer.

The problem requires s∈(−2,2), hence
  s ∈ {−1,0,1}.
Also s≠r (two distinct roots), so for each fixed r∈{−1,0,1}, there are exactly two choices for s:
  s ∈ {−1,0,1}\setminus\{r\}.

Thus there are 3·2 = 6 ordered pairs (r,s) that meet the interval and distinctness requirements.

Step 4 (construct_coefficients_and_verify_bounds):
For any such pair (r,s), expanding
  (x−r)^2(x−s) = x^3 − (2r+s)x^2 + (r^2+2rs)x − r^2 s
shows the corresponding coefficients are
  a = −(2r+s),   b = r^2+2rs,   c = −r^2 s.

With r,s∈{−1,0,1}, we have 2r+s ∈ [−3,3], r^2+2rs ∈ [−2,3], and −r^2 s ∈ {−1,0,1}, so indeed a,b,c ∈ {−3,…,3} automatically.

Therefore every admissible (r,s) yields a valid triple (a,b,c) in the required coefficient box.

Step 5 (count and (optional) list).
Hence the number of such polynomials equals the number of admissible ordered pairs (r,s), which is 6.

Explicitly, the 6 polynomials are:
  r= 1, s= 0:  P(x)=x(x−1)^2 = x^3 −2x^2 + x        (a,b,c)=(-2, 1, 0)
  r= 1, s=−1:  P(x)=(x−1)^2(x+1)=x^3 −x^2 −x + 1    (a,b,c)=(-1,-1, 1)
  r= 0, s= 1:  P(x)=x^2(x−1)=x^3 −x^2               (a,b,c)=(-1, 0, 0)
  r=−1, s= 1:  P(x)=(x+1)^2(x−1)=x^3 +x^2 −x − 1    (a,b,c)=( 1,-1,-1)
  r= 0, s=−1:  P(x)=x^2(x+1)=x^3 +x^2               (a,b,c)=( 1, 0, 0)
  r=−1, s= 0:  P(x)=x(x+1)^2 = x^3 +2x^2 + x        (a,b,c)=( 2, 1, 0)
Checking:
We show the count is forced, with no missing cases and no double-counting.

(A) Any qualifying polynomial has the form (x−r)^2(x−s) with r≠s.
“Exactly two distinct real roots” for a cubic is equivalent to “one double real root and one simple real root,” so P must factor as (x−r)^2(x−s).

(B) The double root r must be an integer in {−1,0,1}.
Because r is a repeated root, it is a rational root (root of gcd(P,P′)), and since P is monic with integer coefficients, r must be an integer dividing c.
The interval constraint r∈(−2,2) then forces r∈{−1,0,1}.

(C) The simple root s is uniquely determined by (a,r) and must be an integer in {−1,0,1} distinct from r.
Vieta gives s=−a−2r, hence s is an integer. The interval constraint forces s∈{−1,0,1}, and distinctness forces s≠r.
So there are exactly 2 choices of s for each of the 3 choices of r, giving exactly 6 possible (r,s).

(D) Each admissible ordered pair (r,s) determines exactly one coefficient triple (a,b,c), and different pairs give different polynomials.
Expanding (x−r)^2(x−s) determines (a,b,c) uniquely. Conversely, factorization into roots is unique, so two different ordered pairs (r,s) cannot produce the same polynomial.

Thus there are exactly 6 qualifying coefficient triples, uniquely.

Solve the following problem.

Let
  P(x)=x^3 + a x^2 + b x + c
with integers a,b,c ∈ {−4,−3,−2,−1,0,1,2,3,4}. Assume:

1) Forced-root condition: P(0)P(1)=0 (so at least one of 0 or 1 is a root).
2) Squarefree condition: P has no repeated roots in ℂ (equivalently, gcd(P,P′)=1 in ℤ[x]).
3) Uniqueness condition: P has exactly one integer root.

Count how many such polynomials P exist.

Step 1 (split_on_unique_forced_root):
Since P(0)P(1)=0, at least one of 0 or 1 is an integer root. Because P has exactly one integer root, the unique integer root r must be exactly one of
  r=0  or  r=1,
and these cases are disjoint.

We count the admissible polynomials in each case and add.

Step 2 (translate_to_quadratic_constraints):
In each case, factor out the known integer root (integer root factor theorem).

• Case r=0:
  c = P(0)=0 and
  P(x)=xQ(x),   where Q(x)=x^2 + a x + b.

• Case r=1:
  P(1)=0 and
  P(x)=(x−1)Q(x), where Q(x)=x^2 + p x + q for some integers p,q.

The condition “P has exactly one integer root” means: the quadratic cofactor Q has no integer roots.
For a monic quadratic Q(x)=x^2+ux+v, the discriminant
  Δ(Q)=u^2−4v
is an integer, and Q has integer roots iff Δ(Q) is a perfect square (including 0); Q has a repeated root iff Δ(Q)=0.
Therefore, in either case, “Q has no integer roots and P is squarefree” is ensured by the single condition
  Δ(Q) is not a perfect square integer.

(Indeed, Δ(Q)=0 is a square, and if Q had an integer root then Δ(Q) would be a nonnegative square.)

Step 3 (count_coefficients_under_discriminant_filter: the r=0 case):
Here Q(x)=x^2 + a x + b with a,b ∈ [−4,4], and we require
  Δ0 := a^2 − 4b  is not a perfect square.
There are 9·9=81 pairs (a,b). We count and subtract the “bad” pairs where Δ0 is a perfect square.

Fix a and scan b∈[−4,4]. Since Δ0=a^2−4b changes by 4 when b changes by 1, it is easy to list squares.

- a=0:  Δ0=−4b ∈ {16,12,8,4,0,−4,−8,−12,−16}. Squares occur at 16,4,0:
  b=−4,−1,0  → 3 bad b’s.
- a=±1: a^2=1, so Δ0=1−4b ∈ {17,13,9,5,1,−3,−7,−11,−15}. Squares occur at 9,1:
  b=−2,0  → 2 bad b’s for each of a=1 and a=−1.
- a=±2: a^2=4, so Δ0=4−4b=4(1−b). This is a square iff 1−b is a square in {0,1,4}:
  b=1,0,−3  → 3 bad b’s for each of a=2 and a=−2.
- a=±3: a^2=9, so Δ0=9−4b ∈ {25,21,17,13,9,5,1,−3,−7}. Squares occur at 25,9,1:
  b=−4,0,2  → 3 bad b’s for each of a=3 and a=−3.
- a=±4: a^2=16, so Δ0=16−4b ∈ {32,28,24,20,16,12,8,4,0}. Squares occur at 16,4,0:
  b=0,3,4  → 3 bad b’s for each of a=4 and a=−4.

Total bad pairs:
  a=±1 contribute 2+2 = 4,
  the remaining 7 values of a contribute 3 each, i.e. 7·3 = 21,
so bad = 4+21 = 25.
Hence good pairs (a,b) are
  81 − 25 = 56.

Therefore the r=0 case contributes 56 polynomials.

Step 4 (count_coefficients_under_discriminant_filter: the r=1 case):
Write
  P(x)=(x−1)(x^2 + p x + q).
Expanding gives
  P(x)=x^3 + (p−1)x^2 + (q−p)x − q,
so
  a = p−1,   b = q−p,   c = −q.
Thus (a,b,c) lie in [−4,4]^3 exactly when
  p ∈ [−3,5],   q ∈ [−4,4],   q−p ∈ [−4,4].
This change of variables is a bijection between admissible coefficient triples with root 1 and integer pairs (p,q) satisfying those bounds.

The quadratic cofactor is Q(x)=x^2 + p x + q, with discriminant
  Δ1 := p^2 − 4q,
which must NOT be a perfect square.

Now count integer pairs (p,q) with:
  p ∈ {−3,−2,−1,0,1,2,3,4,5},
  q ∈ [−4,4] ∩ [p−4, p+4]     (equivalently q−p ∈ [−4,4]),
and with Δ1 not a square.

p = −3:
  q = −4,…,1  (6 values)
  Δ1 = 9−4q = 25,21,17,13,9,5  → squares: 25,9 (2)
  good: 6−2 = 4

p = −2:
  q = −4,…,2  (7 values)
  Δ1 = 4−4q = 20,16,12,8,4,0,−4  → squares: 16,4,0 (3)
  good: 7−3 = 4

p = −1:
  q = −4,…,3  (8 values)
  Δ1 = 1−4q = 17,13,9,5,1,−3,−7,−11  → squares: 9,1 (2)
  good: 8−2 = 6

p = 0:
  q = −4,…,4  (9 values)
  Δ1 = −4q = 16,12,8,4,0,−4,−8,−12,−16  → squares: 16,4,0 (3)

p = 1:
  q = −3,…,4  (8 values)

p = 2:

p = 3:

p = 4:

p = 5:
  q = 1,…,4  (4 values)
  Δ1 = 25−4q = 21,17,13,9  → squares: 9 (1)
  good: 4−1 = 3

Summing the good counts:
  4+4+6+6+6+5+4+2+3 = 40.

Therefore the r=1 case contributes 40 polynomials.

Step 5 (sum_cases):
Total number of polynomials is
  56 + 40 = 96.

Solve the following problem.

Let $M=11^5\cdot 19^2\cdot 2^{10}$.

Let $T$ be the unique integer such that $0\le T<M$ and
$$
T\equiv 1+4\cdot 11^2 \pmod{11^5},\qquad
T\equiv 2 \pmod{19^2},\qquad
T\equiv 3 \pmod{2^{10}}.
$$

Let $t$ be the smallest positive integer such that
$$
11^5\mid (T^t-1),\qquad 19^2\mid (T^t-1),\qquad 2^{10}\mid (T^t-1).
$$

Define $A$ by
$$
A\equiv \frac{T^t-1}{11^5}+\frac{T^t-1}{19^2}+\frac{T^t-1}{2^{10}}\pmod{1000},
$$
where each division is taken in the integers. Let $N$ be the unique integer in $\{0,1,\dots,999\}$ such that $N\equiv A\pmod{1000}$.
Determine $N$.

Step 1. (CRT well-definedness of $T$)
The moduli $11^5$, $19^2$, and $2^{10}$ are pairwise coprime, so the Chinese Remainder Theorem gives a unique residue class modulo $M=11^5\cdot 19^2\cdot 2^{10}$ satisfying the three congruences, and the normalization $0\le T<M$ selects a unique integer $T$.

Step 2. (Explicit computation of $T$ by CRT)
Compute the residues:
$11^5=161051$, $19^2=361$, and $1+4\cdot 11^2=485$.

Write $T=485+161051k$ and impose $T\equiv 2\pmod{361}$.
Reducing modulo $361$ gives $485\equiv 124$ and $161051\equiv 45$, hence
$124+45k\equiv 2\pmod{361}$, so $45k\equiv 239\pmod{361}$.
Since $361=45\cdot 8+1$, we have $45^{-1}\equiv -8\equiv 353\pmod{361}$, hence
$k\equiv 239\cdot 353\equiv 254\pmod{361}$.
So $k=254+361m$ and therefore
$T=485+161051(254+361m)=40907439+58139411\,m$.

Now impose $T\equiv 3\pmod{1024}$:
$40907439+58139411m\equiv 3\pmod{1024}$.
Reducing modulo $1024$ gives $40907439\equiv 687$ and $58139411\equiv 787$, so
$687+787m\equiv 3\pmod{1024}$, i.e. $787m\equiv 340\pmod{1024}$.
A Euclidean back-substitution yields $787^{-1}\equiv 795\pmod{1024}$, hence
$m\equiv 340\cdot 795\equiv 988\pmod{1024}$.
Taking $m=988$ gives
$T=40907439+58139411\cdot 988=57482645507$.
Also $M=58139411\cdot 1024=59534756864$, so indeed $0\le T<M$.

Step 3. (A valuation lemma for $a^n-1$ when $p\mid (a-1)$)
Lemma 1. Let $p$ be an odd prime and let $a$ be an integer with $p\mid (a-1)$ and $p\nmid a$. Then for every $n\ge 1$,
$$
v_p(a^n-1)=v_p(a-1)+v_p(n).
$$

Proof.
Write $n=p^k m$ where $k=v_p(n)$ and $p\nmid m$.

First, assume $p\nmid m$. Factor
$a^m-1=(a-1)(a^{m-1}+a^{m-2}+\cdots+1)$.
Since $a\equiv 1\pmod{p}$, the sum satisfies
$a^{m-1}+\cdots+1\equiv m\pmod{p}$, and $p\nmid m$ implies $p\nmid (a^{m-1}+\cdots+1)$.
Hence $v_p(a^m-1)=v_p(a-1)$.

Next, prove by induction on $k\ge 0$ that $v_p(a^{p^k}-1)=v_p(a-1)+k$.
For $k=0$ this is tautological.
Assume it holds for $k$ and set $b=a^{p^k}$, so $v_p(b-1)=v_p(a-1)+k\ge 1$.
Expand with the binomial theorem:
$b^p-1=(1+(b-1))^p-1=p(b-1)+\sum_{j=2}^p \binom{p}{j}(b-1)^j$.
The first term has valuation $v_p(p(b-1))=1+v_p(b-1)$.
For $j\ge 2$, $\binom{p}{j}$ is divisible by $p$ but not by $p^2$, so $v_p\!\left(\binom{p}{j}\right)=1$, and thus
$v_p\!\left(\binom{p}{j}(b-1)^j\right)\ge 1+2v_p(b-1)>1+v_p(b-1)$.
Therefore the first term has strictly smaller $p$-adic valuation than every other term, so
$v_p(b^p-1)=1+v_p(b-1)$.
Hence $v_p(a^{p^{k+1}}-1)=v_p(b^p-1)=1+v_p(b-1)=v_p(a-1)+(k+1)$.

Finally, with $n=p^k m$ and $p\nmid m$, we have
$a^n-1=(a^{p^k})^m-1$.
Applying the first part with $a^{p^k}$ in place of $a$ and $m$ in place of $n$ gives
$v_p(a^n-1)=v_p(a^{p^k}-1)=v_p(a-1)+k=v_p(a-1)+v_p(n)$.
This proves the lemma.  $\square$

Step 4. (Compute $\operatorname{ord}_{11^5}(T)$)
From the defining congruence, $T\equiv 1+4\cdot 11^2\pmod{11^5}$, hence $v_{11}(T-1)=2$.
Applying Lemma 1 with $p=11$ and $a=T$ gives
$v_{11}(T^n-1)=2+v_{11}(n)$.
Therefore $11^5\mid (T^n-1)$ is equivalent to $2+v_{11}(n)\ge 5$, i.e. $v_{11}(n)\ge 3$, i.e. $11^3\mid n$.
Thus the smallest positive $n$ with $T^n\equiv 1\pmod{11^5}$ is $11^3=1331$, so
$\operatorname{ord}_{11^5}(T)=1331$.

Step 5. (Compute $\operatorname{ord}_{19}(T)$)
Because $T\equiv 2\pmod{19^2}$, we also have $T\equiv 2\pmod{19}$.

Solve the following problem.

Let $p=41$ and $q=23$. Let $a$ and $b$ be positive integers with $a>b$, $a^2+b^2=p$, and $a=b+1$. Let $T=\left(\frac{q}{p}\right)$ be the Legendre symbol. Let $s$ be the unique integer with $0\le s<p$ and
$$s\equiv a+Tb\pmod p.$$

Let $M=64\,p^4$ and define
$$S=\Bigl\{(x,y)\in(\mathbb{Z}/M\mathbb{Z})^2:\ x^2+y^2\equiv p\pmod M,\ x\equiv sy\pmod p,\ x\equiv sy+1\pmod{64}\Bigr\}.$$
Let $N=|S|$.

Determine the unique integer $A\in\{0,1,\dots,996\}$ such that $A\equiv N\pmod{997}$.

Step 1. Determine $(a,b)$.
From $a=b+1$ and $a^2+b^2=41$,
$$(b+1)^2+b^2=41 \;\Longrightarrow\; 2b^2+2b+1=41 \;\Longrightarrow\; b^2+b-20=0.$$
The discriminant is $1+80=81$, so $b=\frac{-1\pm 9}{2}\in\{4,-5\}$. Since $b>0$, we get $b=4$ and $a=5$.

Step 2. Compute $T=\left(\frac{23}{41}\right)$.
Since $41\equiv 1\pmod 4$, quadratic reciprocity gives
$$\left(\frac{23}{41}\right)=\left(\frac{41}{23}\right).$$
Reduce $41$ modulo $23$: $41\equiv 18\pmod{23}$. Thus
$$\left(\frac{41}{23}\right)=\left(\frac{18}{23}\right)=\left(\frac{2}{23}\right)\left(\frac{9}{23}\right).$$
Now $\left(\frac{9}{23}\right)=1$ (because $9$ is a square), and
$$\left(\frac{2}{23}\right)=(-1)^{(23^2-1)/8}=(-1)^{66}=1.$$
Hence $T=1$.

Step 3. Compute $s$ and the key identity $s^2+1=2p$.
By definition $s\equiv a+Tb\pmod p$ with $0\le s<p$. Using $a=5$, $b=4$, $T=1$:
$$s\equiv 5+4\equiv 9\pmod{41},\qquad 0\le s<41,$$
so $s=9$.

Lemma 1 (consecutive sum-of-two-squares identity).
If $p=a^2+b^2$ and $a=b+1$, then $(a+b)^2+1=2p$ as an exact integer identity.
Proof.
Let $u=a+b$. With $a=b+1$ we have $u=2b+1$, so
$$u^2+1=(2b+1)^2+1=4b^2+4b+2=2\bigl((b+1)^2+b^2\bigr)=2p.$$
This proves Lemma 1.

In our instance, $a+b=9=s$, so Lemma 1 gives the exact identity
$$s^2+1=2p.$$

Step 4. Split the counting problem into a $p$-power part and a $2$-power part.
Write $M=64\cdot p^4$ with $\gcd(64,p^4)=1$. Define
$$S_p=\Bigl\{(x,y)\in(\mathbb{Z}/p^4\mathbb{Z})^2:\ x^2+y^2\equiv p\pmod{p^4},\ x\equiv sy\pmod p\Bigr\},$$
$$S_2=\Bigl\{(x,y)\in(\mathbb{Z}/64\mathbb{Z})^2:\ x^2+y^2\equiv p\pmod{64},\ x\equiv sy+1\pmod{64}\Bigr\}.$$

Lemma 2 (CRT product bijection).
The reduction map modulo $p^4$ and modulo $64$ gives a bijection $S\cong S_p\times S_2$. Hence $N=|S|=|S_p|\cdot |S_2|$.
Proof.
Since $\gcd(p^4,64)=1$, the Chinese remainder theorem gives a bijection
$$(\mathbb{Z}/M\mathbb{Z})^2\cong (\mathbb{Z}/p^4\mathbb{Z})^2\times(\mathbb{Z}/64\mathbb{Z})^2.$$
The condition $x^2+y^2\equiv p\pmod M$ is equivalent to the pair of congruences modulo $p^4$ and modulo $64$, and the slice constraints are separated: $x\equiv sy\pmod p$ is read entirely in the $p$-power component, while $x\equiv sy+1\pmod{64}$ is read entirely in the $2$-power component. Therefore solutions modulo $M$ correspond exactly to pairs of solutions in $S_p$ and $S_2$, and cardinalities multiply.

Step 5. A valuation filter for the $p$-adic component.
Lemma 3.
If $(x,y)\in S_p$, then $p\nmid y$.
Proof.
If $p\mid y$, then $x\equiv sy\pmod p$ implies $p\mid x$. Hence $p^2\mid x^2+y^2$. But $x^2+y^2\equiv p\pmod{p^4}$ implies in particular $x^2+y^2\equiv p\pmod{p^2}$, contradiction since $p\not\equiv 0\pmod{p^2}$. Thus $p\nmid y$.

Step 6. Parametrize the slice $x\equiv sy\pmod p$ modulo $p^4$.
The congruence $x\equiv sy\pmod p$ means $p\mid(x-sy)$, so there is a unique residue class $k\in\mathbb{Z}/p^3\mathbb{Z}$ such that
$$x\equiv sy+pk\pmod{p^4}.$$

Step 7. Reduce $x^2+y^2\equiv p\pmod{p^4}$ to one congruence modulo $p^3$.
Substitute $x\equiv sy+pk\pmod{p^4}$:
$$x^2+y^2\equiv (sy+pk)^2+y^2\equiv (s^2+1)y^2+2sypk+p^2k^2\pmod{p^4}.$$
Using Lemma 1 (in the form $s^2+1=2p$), we get the exact identity
$$x^2+y^2\equiv 2py^2+2sypk+p^2k^2=p\bigl(2y^2+2syk+pk^2\bigr)\pmod{p^4}.$$
Therefore $x^2+y^2\equiv p\pmod{p^4}$ holds if and only if
$$2y^2+2syk+pk^2\equiv 1\pmod{p^3}.$$
Call this congruence $(\star_p)$.

Step 8. Solve $(\star_p)$ modulo $p$.
Reducing $(\star_p)$ modulo $p$ removes the term $pk^2$ and gives
$$2y^2+2syk\equiv 1\pmod p.$$
By Lemma 3, $y\not\equiv 0\pmod p$, and clearly $s\not\equiv 0\pmod p$. Hence $2sy$ is invertible modulo $p$, so for each $y_0\in(\mathbb{Z}/p\mathbb{Z})^\*$ there is a unique $k_0\in\mathbb{Z}/p\mathbb{Z}$ solving this linear congruence.

Step 9. Hensel lifting in $k$ from modulus $p$ to modulus $p^3$.
Fix $y_1\in\mathbb{Z}/p^3\mathbb{Z}$ with $p\nmid y_1$ and define
$$F(k)=2y_1^2+2sy_1k+pk^2-1.$$
Then $(\star_p)$ is exactly $F(k)\equiv 0\pmod{p^3}$.

Lemma 4 (simple-root Hensel lift to $p^3$).
For each fixed $y_1\pmod{p^3}$ with $p\nmid y_1$, there exists a unique $k\pmod{p^3}$ such that $F(k)\equiv 0\pmod{p^3}$.
Proof.
Let $y_0\equiv y_1\pmod p$ (so $y_0\ne 0$). Modulo $p$, the congruence $F(k)\equiv 0\pmod p$ becomes the linear congruence from Step 8, so there is a unique $k_0\pmod p$ with $F(k_0)\equiv 0\pmod p$.

Compute the derivative $F'(k)=2sy_1+2pk$. Modulo $p$ this is $F'(k)\equiv 2sy_0\pmod p$, which is nonzero, hence invertible modulo $p$.

We now lift iteratively.

Inductive lifting step: suppose $n\in\{1,2\}$ and we have $k_n$ with $F(k_n)\equiv 0\pmod{p^n}$. Write $k_{n+1}=k_n+p^n t$ with $t\in\mathbb{Z}/p\mathbb{Z}$. Expand modulo $p^{n+1}$:
$$F(k_n+p^n t)\equiv F(k_n)+p^n t\,F'(k_n)\pmod{p^{n+1}},$$
because all remaining terms contain $(p^n t)^2$ and hence are multiples of $p^{2n}\subseteq p^{n+1}$ for $n\ge 1$.
Write $F(k_n)=p^n E$ for some integer $E$. The condition $F(k_{n+1})\equiv 0\pmod{p^{n+1}}$ becomes
$$E+tF'(k_n)\equiv 0\pmod p.$$
Since $F'(k_n)\equiv F'(k_0)\not\equiv 0\pmod p$, there is a unique $t\pmod p$, hence a unique $k_{n+1}\pmod{p^{n+1}}$.

Starting from the unique $k_1=k_0\pmod p$, we obtain unique lifts to $k_2\pmod{p^2}$ and then $k_3\pmod{p^3}$. This proves Lemma 4.

Step 10. Lift $y$ from modulo $p^3$ to modulo $p^4$.
Lemma 5.
If $(\star_p)$ holds for $(y_1,k)\pmod{p^3}$, then it also holds for $(y_1+p^3 t,k)\pmod{p^3}$ for any integer $t$. Consequently each $y_1\pmod{p^3}$ has exactly $p$ lifts to $y\pmod{p^4}$ that preserve solvability of $x^2+y^2\equiv p\pmod{p^4}$ with the same $k\pmod{p^3}$.
Proof.
In $(\star_p)$ we work modulo $p^3$. Replacing $y_1$ by $y_1+p^3 t$ does not change $y_1$ modulo $p^3$, hence does not change $y_1^2$ modulo $p^3$ nor $y_1k$ modulo $p^3$. Thus the congruence remains true.
For lifts modulo $p^4$, each residue class modulo $p^3$ has exactly $p$ lifts.

Step 11. Count $|S_p|$.
There are $\varphi(p^3)=p^2(p-1)$ residue classes $y_1\pmod{p^3}$ with $p\nmid y_1$. For each such $y_1$, Lemma 4 gives exactly one $k\pmod{p^3}$, hence exactly one $x\pmod{p^4}$ on the slice $x\equiv sy+pk\pmod{p^4}$. By Lemma 5, each $y_1$ gives $p$ lifts to $y\pmod{p^4}$.

Therefore
$$|S_p|=p\cdot \varphi(p^3)=p\cdot p^2(p-1)=p^3(p-1).$$
With $p=41$, $41^3=68921$, so
$$|S_p|=68921\cdot 40=2{,}756{,}840.$$

Step 12. Reduce the $2$-adic component $S_2$ to a quadratic congruence in $y$.
In $S_2$, the constraint $x\equiv sy+1\pmod{64}$ means we may substitute $x\equiv sy+1$ into $x^2+y^2\equiv p\pmod{64}$:
$$(sy+1)^2+y^2\equiv p\pmod{64}.$$
Expanding and using $s^2+1=2p$,
$$(s^2+1)y^2+2sy+1-p\equiv 0\pmod{64}$$
becomes
$$2py^2+2sy+1-p\equiv 0\pmod{64}.$$
All terms are even, so dividing by $2$ gives the equivalent congruence
$$py^2+sy+\frac{1-p}{2}\equiv 0\pmod{32}.$$
Let $c=\frac{1-p}{2}$.

Step 13. Complete the square (modulo $128$).
Lemma 6.
The congruence $py^2+sy+c\equiv 0\pmod{32}$ is equivalent to
$$(2py+s)^2\equiv s^2-4pc\pmod{128}.$$
Proof.
Compute
$$(2py+s)^2=4p^2y^2+4psy+s^2=4p(py^2+sy)+s^2.$$
Therefore
$$(2py+s)^2-(s^2-4pc)=4p(py^2+sy+c).$$
Hence $py^2+sy+c\equiv 0\pmod{32}$ holds if and only if the right-hand side is divisible by $4p\cdot 32=128$, i.e. if and only if $(2py+s)^2\equiv s^2-4pc\pmod{128}$.

Step 14. Evaluate the discriminant $D=s^2-4pc$.
We have $c=\frac{1-p}{2}$, so
$$D=s^2-4p\cdot \frac{1-p}{2}=s^2-2p(1-p)=s^2-2p+2p^2.$$
Using $s^2+1=2p$, i.e. $s^2=2p-1$, we obtain
$$D=(2p-1)-2p+2p^2=2p^2-1.$$
For $p=41$, this is $D=2\cdot 41^2-1=3361$.

Also, since $p$ is odd, $p^2\equiv 1\pmod 8$, so
$$D=2p^2-1\equiv 2\cdot 1-1\equiv 1\pmod 8.$$

Step 15. Count square roots of $D$ modulo $128$.
From Step 14 we know $D=3361$. Reducing modulo $128$ gives $D\equiv 33\pmod{128}$.

First we show that $D$ is actually a square modulo $128$ in a controlled way.
Let $z_0=2p-1=81$ (an odd residue, hence invertible modulo $128$). Then
$$z_0^2-(2p^2-1)=(2p-1)^2-(2p^2-1)=2(p-1)^2.$$
Here $p-1=40$ is divisible by $8$, so $(p-1)^2$ is divisible by $64$, hence $2(p-1)^2$ is divisible by $128$.
Therefore $z_0^2\equiv 2p^2-1=D\pmod{128}$, so $z^2\equiv D\pmod{128}$ has at least one solution.

Lemma 7 (exactly four square roots modulo $128$ once one exists).
Let $U$ be odd, and suppose there exists an odd residue $z_0$ with $z_0^2\equiv U\pmod{128}$.
Then the congruence $z^2\equiv U\pmod{128}$ has exactly $4$ solutions modulo $128$.
Proof.
Because $z_0$ is odd, it is invertible modulo $128$.
If $z^2\equiv U\pmod{128}$, then $(zz_0^{-1})^2\equiv 1\pmod{128}$.
Conversely, if $w^2\equiv 1\pmod{128}$ then $(z_0w)^2\equiv z_0^2\equiv U\pmod{128}$.
So the solutions to $z^2\equiv U\pmod{128}$ are exactly $z=z_0w$ where $w$ runs over the solutions to $w^2\equiv 1\pmod{128}$.
Thus it suffices to count the solutions of $w^2\equiv 1\pmod{128}$.

Solve $w^2\equiv 1\pmod{128}$:
$$w^2\equiv 1\pmod{128}\ \Longleftrightarrow\ 128\mid (w-1)(w+1).$$
Since any such $w$ must be odd, $\gcd(w-1,w+1)=2$.
Write $w-1=2A$ and $w+1=2B$ with $\gcd(A,B)=1$. Then
$$128\mid (w-1)(w+1)=4AB \ \Longleftrightarrow\ 32\mid AB.$$
Because $\gcd(A,B)=1$, this forces $32\mid A$ or $32\mid B$.

If $32\mid A$, then $w-1$ is divisible by $64$, so $w\equiv 1\pmod{64}$, i.e. $w\equiv 1$ or $65\pmod{128}$.
If $32\mid B$, then $w+1$ is divisible by $64$, so $w\equiv -1\pmod{64}$, i.e. $w\equiv 127$ or $63\pmod{128}$.

These four residues $1,65,63,127$ indeed satisfy $w^2\equiv 1\pmod{128}$, so there are exactly $4$ solutions for $w$ and hence exactly $4$ solutions for $z$.

Applying this lemma to $U=D$ (where we already exhibited a solution $z_0$) shows that $z^2\equiv D\pmod{128}$ has exactly $4$ solutions.

Finally, by Lemma 6 the map $y\pmod{64}\mapsto z\equiv 2py+s\pmod{128}$ is a bijection between solutions in $S_2$ and solutions to $z^2\equiv D\pmod{128}$ (since $p$ is invertible modulo $64$).
Therefore $|S_2|=4$.

Step 16. Combine counts and reduce modulo $997$.
By Lemma 2,
$$N=|S|=|S_p|\cdot |S_2|=2{,}756{,}840\cdot 4=11{,}027{,}360.$$
Reduce modulo $997$:
$$11{,}027{,}360-997\cdot 11{,}000=60{,}360,$$
$$60{,}360-997\cdot 60=540.$$
Hence $N\equiv 540\pmod{997}$, so the required $A$ is
$$A=540.$$

Solve the following problem.

Let $p=61$ and $q=19$. Let $a$ and $b$ be positive integers with $a>b$, $a^2+b^2=p$, and $a=b+1$. Let $T$ be the Legendre symbol $(\frac{q}{p})$. Let $s$ be the unique integer with $0\le s<p$ and $s\equiv a+Tb\pmod p$.

Let $S$ be the set of pairs $(x,y)\in(\mathbb{Z}/p^3\mathbb{Z})^2$ such that $x^2+y^2\equiv p\pmod{p^3}$ and $x\equiv sy\pmod p$. Let $N=|S|$.

Determine the unique integer $A\in\{0,1,\dots,998\}$ such that $A\equiv N\pmod{999}$.

Step 1. Solve for $(a,b)$ and record the identity forced by $a=b+1$.
From $a=b+1$ and $a^2+b^2=p$ we get $(b+1)^2+b^2=p$, so $2b^2+2b+1=61$ and $b^2+b-30=0$. The discriminant is $1+120=121=11^2$, so
$b=\frac{-1\pm 11}{2}$.
The negative root is $b=-6$, which is impossible since $b>0$. Hence $b=5$ and $a=6$.

Define $u=a+b=11$.

Lemma 1. With $a=b+1$ and $u=a+b$, one has the exact integer identity $u^2+1=2p$, and moreover $0<u<p$.
Proof. Since $a=b+1$, we have $u=2b+1$, so
$u^2+1=(2b+1)^2+1=4b^2+4b+2=2(2b^2+2b+1)=2p$.
Also $p-u=(2b^2+2b+1)-(2b+1)=2b^2>0$, so $u<p$. Clearly $u>0$. This proves Lemma 1.

In our instance, $u=11$ and $u^2+1=122=2\cdot 61$.

Step 2. Compute $T=(\frac{q}{p})=(\frac{19}{61})$ by quadratic reciprocity.
Since $61\equiv 1\pmod 4$, quadratic reciprocity gives $(\frac{19}{61})=(\frac{61}{19})$.
Now $61\equiv 4\pmod{19}$, hence $(\frac{61}{19})=(\frac{4}{19})=1$ because $4$ is a square modulo every odd prime.
Therefore $T=1$.

Step 3. Compute $s$ and the key identity for $s$.
By definition $s\equiv a+Tb\pmod p$ with $0\le s<p$. Using $a=6$, $b=5$, and $T=1$, we get
$s\equiv 6+5\equiv 11\pmod{61}$, hence $s=11$.

Because $T=1$, we have $s\equiv a+b\pmod p$, and Lemma 1 gives $0<u<p$, so $s=u$. In particular,
$s^2+1=u^2+1=2p$ as an exact integer identity.

Step 4. Show that every solution in $S$ satisfies $p\nmid y$.
Let $(x,y)\in S$. If $p\mid y$, then from $x\equiv sy\pmod p$ we also get $p\mid x$. Then $p^2\mid x^2+y^2$, so $x^2+y^2\equiv 0\pmod{p^2}$.
But $x^2+y^2\equiv p\pmod{p^3}$ implies $x^2+y^2\equiv p\pmod{p^2}$, a contradiction since $p\not\equiv 0\pmod{p^2}$.
Thus $p\nmid y$ for all $(x,y)\in S$.

Step 5. Parametrize the slice condition $x\equiv sy\pmod p$ modulo $p^3$.
The congruence $x\equiv sy\pmod p$ is equivalent to $p\mid (x-sy)$, so there exists $k\in\mathbb{Z}/p^2\mathbb{Z}$ such that
$x\equiv sy+pk\pmod{p^3}$.
This residue class $k\pmod{p^2}$ is unique, because if $pk\equiv pk'\pmod{p^3}$ then $p^3\mid p(k-k')$, hence $p^2\mid (k-k')$, so $k\equiv k'\pmod{p^2}$.

Step 6. Reduce $x^2+y^2\equiv p\pmod{p^3}$ to a single congruence modulo $p^2$.
Substitute $x\equiv sy+pk\pmod{p^3}$:
$x^2+y^2\equiv (sy+pk)^2+y^2\equiv (s^2+1)y^2+2sypk+p^2k^2\pmod{p^3}$.
Using Step 3, $s^2+1=2p$, so
$x^2+y^2\equiv 2py^2+2sypk+p^2k^2=p(2y^2+2syk+pk^2)\pmod{p^3}$.

Therefore $x^2+y^2\equiv p\pmod{p^3}$ holds if and only if
$p(2y^2+2syk+pk^2)\equiv p\pmod{p^3}$,
which is equivalent to $p^3\mid p(2y^2+2syk+pk^2-1)$, hence to
$2y^2+2syk+pk^2\equiv 1\pmod{p^2}$.
Call this congruence $(\star)$.

Step 7. Solve $(\star)$ modulo $p$ to obtain $k$ modulo $p$ uniquely for each $y$ modulo $p$.
Reducing $(\star)$ modulo $p$ gives $2y^2+2syk\equiv 1\pmod p$.
By Step 4, $y\not\equiv 0\pmod p$, and also $s\not\equiv 0\pmod p$, so $2sy$ is invertible modulo $p$.
Hence for each $y_0\in(\mathbb{Z}/p\mathbb{Z})^*$ there is a unique $k_0\in\mathbb{Z}/p\mathbb{Z}$ with
$2y_0^2+2sy_0k_0\equiv 1\pmod p$.

Step 8. Recast $(\star)$ as a lifting problem in $k$ at modulus $p^2$.
Fix $y_1\in\mathbb{Z}/p^2\mathbb{Z}$ with $p\nmid y_1$, and define
$F(k)=2y_1^2+2sy_1k+pk^2-1$.
Then $(\star)$ is exactly $F(k)\equiv 0\pmod{p^2}$.
Modulo $p$ this becomes the linear congruence from Step 7, so there is a unique $k_0\pmod p$ with $F(k_0)\equiv 0\pmod p$.

Step 9. Uniquely lift $k_0$ modulo $p$ to a solution modulo $p^2$.
Lemma 2. Fix $y_1\in\mathbb{Z}/p^2\mathbb{Z}$ with $p\nmid y_1$, and let $y_0\equiv y_1\pmod p$. Let $k_0\in\mathbb{Z}/p\mathbb{Z}$ be the unique solution of $2y_0^2+2sy_0k\equiv 1\pmod p$. Then there exists a unique $k\in\mathbb{Z}/p^2\mathbb{Z}$ with $k\equiv k_0\pmod p$ and $F(k)\equiv 0\pmod{p^2}$.
Proof. Write $k=k_0+pt$ with $t\in\mathbb{Z}/p\mathbb{Z}$. Let $B=F(k_0)$ viewed as an integer representative. Since $F(k_0)\equiv 0\pmod p$, we can write $B=pE$ for some integer $E$.

Now expand:
$F(k_0+pt)=2y_1^2+2sy_1(k_0+pt)+p(k_0+pt)^2-1$.
Modulo $p^2$, the term $p(k_0+pt)^2$ is congruent to $pk_0^2$, because $(k_0+pt)^2=k_0^2+2k_0pt+p^2t^2$ and the last two terms become multiples of $p^2$ after multiplying by $p$. Hence
$F(k_0+pt)\equiv F(k_0)+2sy_1pt\equiv pE+2sy_1pt\pmod{p^2}$.

Therefore $F(k_0+pt)\equiv 0\pmod{p^2}$ is equivalent to $pE+2sy_1pt\equiv 0\pmod{p^2}$, which is equivalent to
$E+2sy_0t\equiv 0\pmod p$
because $y_1\equiv y_0\pmod p$.
Since $y_0\not\equiv 0\pmod p$ and $s\not\equiv 0\pmod p$, the coefficient $2sy_0$ is invertible modulo $p$, so there is a unique $t\pmod p$, hence a unique $k\pmod{p^2}$. This proves Lemma 2.

Thus for each $y_1\pmod{p^2}$ with $p\nmid y_1$, there is exactly one $k\pmod{p^2}$ satisfying $(\star)$.

Step 10. Count solutions modulo $p^3$.
There are $p^2$ residues modulo $p^2$, and exactly $p$ of them are divisible by $p$, so the number of $y_1\pmod{p^2}$ with $p\nmid y_1$ is $p^2-p=p(p-1)=\varphi(p^2)$.

For each such $y_1$, Lemma 2 gives a unique $k\pmod{p^2}$, hence a unique $x\pmod{p^3}$ from $x\equiv sy+pk\pmod{p^3}$.

Now lift $y$ from modulo $p^2$ to modulo $p^3$. Each fixed residue $y_1\pmod{p^2}$ has exactly $p$ lifts $y\pmod{p^3}$ of the form $y=y_1+p^2u$ with $u\in\mathbb{Z}/p\mathbb{Z}$. The congruence $(\star)$ is modulo $p^2$, so it depends only on $y\pmod{p^2}$ and is unchanged by adding $p^2u$. Therefore the same $k$ works for all $p$ lifts.

Hence each admissible $y_1$ contributes exactly $p$ solutions $(x,y)\pmod{p^3}$, and
$N=p\cdot \varphi(p^2)=p\cdot p(p-1)=p^2(p-1)$.

Step 11. Verify the alternative sign $T=-1$ would force $N=0$.
If $T=-1$, then $s\equiv a-b\pmod p$, and since $a=b+1$ we have $a-b=1$, so $s=1$.
Then the slice is $x\equiv y\pmod p$. Reducing $x^2+y^2\equiv p\pmod{p^3}$ modulo $p$ gives $x^2+y^2\equiv 0\pmod p$.
With $x\equiv y\pmod p$ this becomes $2y^2\equiv 0\pmod p$, hence $y\equiv 0\pmod p$, contradicting Step 4. So the set $S$ is empty when $T=-1$.

In our instance Step 2 gave $T=1$, so the nonempty count from Step 10 applies.

Step 12. Compute $A$.
With $p=61$, Step 10 gives $N=p^2(p-1)=61^2\cdot 60=3721\cdot 60=223260$.
Reduce modulo $999$: $223260-999\cdot 223=223260-222777=483$.
Thus the unique $A\in\{0,1,\dots,998\}$ with $A\equiv N\pmod{999}$ is $A=483$.

- Check that any case split considered all possibilities and that no other admissible case gives a different result.

$u^2\equiv -1\pmod p$.

integers $a,b$ iff $p\equiv 1\pmod 4$. Here $61\equiv 1\pmod 4$, so such representations exist. Moreover, in $\mathbb{Z}[i]$,
$p=(a+ib)(a-ib)$ and for primes $p\equiv 1\pmod 4$ this factorization is unique up to multiplying by a unit in $\{\pm 1,\pm i\}$
and swapping $a,b$; therefore the normalization $a>b>0$ makes $(a,b)$ unique. This canonicalizes $(a,b)$ for the later coupling.

Step 3 (Legendre_symbol): For an odd prime $p$ and integer $t$ with $p\nmid t$, the Legendre symbol $\left(\frac{t}{p}\right)$ equals
$1$ if $t$ is a nonzero square mod $p$ and equals $-1$ otherwise. Euler's criterion states
$\left(\frac{t}{p}\right)\equiv t^{(p-1)/2}\pmod p$, so $\left(\frac{t}{p}\right)\in\{\pm 1\}$. Since $q<p$ are distinct primes,
$T=\left(\frac{q}{p}\right)$ is defined and is a sign.

Step 4 (quadratic_reciprocity): Compute $T=\left(\frac{47}{61}\right)$. By from Step 3 we may evaluate it via quadratic reciprocity and
supplementary laws. Since $61\equiv 1\pmod 4$, reciprocity gives
$\left(\frac{47}{61}\right)=\left(\frac{61}{47}\right)$.
Reduce $61\equiv 14\pmod{47}$, so $T=\left(\frac{14}{47}\right)=\left(\frac{2}{47}\right)\left(\frac{7}{47}\right)$.
The supplementary law for $2$ gives $\left(\frac{2}{r}\right)=1$ for $r\equiv \pm 1\pmod 8$; since $47\equiv 7\equiv -1\pmod 8$,

Now $47\equiv 5\pmod 7$, and the nonzero squares mod $7$ are $1,2,4$, so $5$ is a nonresidue and $\left(\frac{5}{7}\right)=-1$.

Model: conditional on the above information, all remaining deals are equally likely; i.e., among the remaining 25 unseen cards in D\setminus\{\heartsuit Q\}, West receives 12 of them and East receives the other 13, uniformly over all such splits.

South plays a spade-trump contract needing 12 tricks, and (given the fixed North–South holdings) the contract succeeds if and only if West does NOT hold both \spadesuit Q and \spadesuit J.

Let X be the conditional probability the contract succeeds. Write X = A/B in lowest terms with A>0. Compute B−A.

Step 1 (define_conditional_sample_space; hypergeometric_counting):
The defenders’ 26-card pool D is fixed. Since West is known to hold \heartsuit Q, remove that card from uncertainty.
There remain 25 unknown cards in D\setminus\{\heartsuit Q\}. West must receive 12 of these, and East receives the other 13.

Thus the conditional sample space has size
  |Ω| = C(25,12),
since choosing West’s remaining 12 cards determines East’s 13 cards automatically.

Step 2 (translate_success_event_to_unfavorable_ownership; compute_probability):
The contract fails exactly when West holds both \spadesuit Q and \spadesuit J. Since \heartsuit Q is already fixed in West’s hand, this failure event is equivalent to:
  “West’s chosen 12 cards from the remaining 25 include both \spadesuit Q and \spadesuit J.”

Count the number of West-choices that contain both \spadesuit Q and \spadesuit J:
- Fix \spadesuit Q and \spadesuit J in West’s 12-card selection.
- The other 10 cards can be chosen freely from the remaining 23 cards.

So the number of unfavorable deals is
  |F| = C(23,10).

Therefore the failure probability is
  P(fail) = |F|/|Ω| = C(23,10) / C(25,12).

Step 3 (fraction_reduction):
Simplify the ratio:
  C(23,10) / C(25,12)
= [23!/(10!13!)] / [25!/(12!13!)]
= (23! · 12!) / (10! · 25!)
= (12! / 10!) · (23! / 25!)
= (12·11) / (25·24)
= 132/600
= 11/50.

Hence
  P(success) = X = 1 − 11/50 = 39/50.

So A=39 and B=50, and
  B−A = 50 − 39 = 11.
Checking:
- Confirm the fraction is in lowest terms by checking the numerator and denominator are coprime.

Answer: 11
